([2 & 3 Will. 4](/wiki/2_%26_3_Will._4 "2 & 3 Will. 4"). c. *19* Pr.), "an Act to enable the Dean and Chapter of Durham Cathedral to appropriate part of the property of their church to the establishment of a University in connection therewith" that the university came into being. The **University of Durham**, which operates under the [trading name](/wiki/Trading_name "Trading name") of **Durham University**,[[7]](#cite_note-branding-7) is a [collegiate](/wiki/Collegiate_university "Collegiate university") [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Durham, England](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England"), founded by an [Act of Parliament](/wiki/Act_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Act of Parliament (United Kingdom)") in 1832 and incorporated by [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") in 1837. It was the first recognised university to open in England for more than 600 years, after [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") and [Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"), and is thus identified by historians as the [third-oldest university in England](/wiki/Third-oldest_university_in_England_debate "Third-oldest university in England debate").[[8]](#cite_note-8) As a collegiate university, its main functions are divided between the academic departments of the university and its [17 colleges](/wiki/Colleges_of_Durham_University "Colleges of Durham University"). In general, the departments perform research and provide teaching to students, while the colleges are responsible for their domestic arrangements and welfare.

The university is a member of the [Russell Group](/wiki/Russell_Group "Russell Group") of British research universities[[9]](#cite_note-9) and is also affiliated with the regional [N8 Research Partnership](/wiki/N8_Research_Partnership "N8 Research Partnership") and international university groups including the [Matariki Network of Universities](/wiki/Matariki_Network_of_Universities "Matariki Network of Universities") and the [Coimbra Group](/wiki/Coimbra_Group "Coimbra Group"). The university estate includes 83 [listed buildings](/wiki/Listed_buildings "Listed buildings"), ranging from the 11th-century [Durham Castle](/wiki/Durham_Castle "Durham Castle") to the 1960s [brutalist](/wiki/Brutalist "Brutalist") [students' union](/wiki/Dunelm_House "Dunelm House"). The university also owns and manages the Durham [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in partnership with [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral"). The university's ownership of the world heritage site includes Durham Castle, [Palace Green](/wiki/Palace_Green "Palace Green") and the surrounding buildings including the historic [Cosin's Library](/wiki/Cosin%27s_Library "Cosin's Library").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Durham graduates have long used the Latin [post-nominal letters](/wiki/Post-nominal_letters "Post-nominal letters") *Dunelm* after their degree, from *Dunelmensis* (of, belonging to, or from Durham).[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Durham University](/wiki/History_of_Durham_University "History of Durham University")

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/William_Van_Mildert_by_Thomas_Lawrence.jpg/250px-William_Van_Mildert_by_Thomas_Lawrence.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Van_Mildert_by_Thomas_Lawrence.jpg)

[William van Mildert](/wiki/William_van_Mildert "William van Mildert"), [Bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham") and one of the founders of the university

Between around 1286 and 1291 the [Benedictine](/wiki/Benedictine "Benedictine") monks of Durham established a hall at the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") to provide them with a seat of learning. In 1381, the hall received an endowment from [Thomas Hatfield](/wiki/Thomas_Hatfield "Thomas Hatfield"), Bishop of Durham, becoming [Durham College](/wiki/Durham_College,_Oxford "Durham College, Oxford"). Durham College was surrendered to the Crown in 1545 following the Reformation. The strong tradition of theological teaching in Durham gave rise to various attempts to form a university within the city itself, notably under King [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England") and then under [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"), who issued [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") and nominated a proctor and fellows for the establishment of a [college in 1657](/wiki/Durham_College_(17th_century) "Durham College (17th century)").[[12]](#cite_note-Penny_Cyclopædia-12) However, a proposal to allow the college to confer degrees met with opposition from Oxford and Cambridge universities, and the whole scheme was abandoned at the restoration of the monarchy in 1660.[[13]](#cite_note-Historic-13) Consequently, it was not until [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"), at the instigation of Archdeacon [Charles Thorp](/wiki/Charles_Thorp "Charles Thorp") and with the support of the bishop of Durham, [William van Mildert](/wiki/William_van_Mildert "William van Mildert"), passed the **Durham University Act 1832** ([2 & 3 Will. 4](/wiki/2_%26_3_Will._4 "2 & 3 Will. 4"). c. *19* Pr.), "an Act to enable the Dean and Chapter of Durham Cathedral to appropriate part of the property of their church to the establishment of a University in connection therewith" that the university came into being. The act received [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") from King [William IV](/wiki/William_IV_of_the_United_Kingdom "William IV of the United Kingdom") on 4 July 1832.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### The church university, 1832–1909

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The church university, 1832–1909")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/ExamInCosin.jpg/250px-ExamInCosin.jpg)](/wiki/File:ExamInCosin.jpg)

An examination taking place in [Cosin's Library](/wiki/Cosin%27s_Library "Cosin's Library"), 1842

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Durham_Castle_Gatehouse_%28geograph_2907552%29.jpg/250px-Durham_Castle_Gatehouse_%28geograph_2907552%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_Castle_Gatehouse_(geograph_2907552).jpg)

[Durham Castle](/wiki/Durham_Castle "Durham Castle") (gatehouse pictured) houses [University College](/wiki/University_College,_Durham "University College, Durham"), making it one of the oldest buildings currently being used to house a university in the world[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The university opened on 28 October 1833.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In 1834 all but two of the bishops of the Church of England confirmed that they would accept holders of Durham degrees for ordination.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In 1835 a fundamental statute was passed by the Dean and Chapter, as governors of the university, setting up [Convocation](/wiki/Convocation "Convocation") and laying down that Durham degrees would only be open to members of the Church of England.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Regulations for degrees were finalised in 1836 and the university was incorporated by [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") granted by William IV on 1 June 1837 as the "Warden, Masters and Scholars of the University of Durham", with the first students graduating a week later.[[13]](#cite_note-Historic-13) At the opening of the university, accommodation was provided in the Archdeacon's Inn (now [Cosin's Hall](/wiki/Cosin%27s_Hall "Cosin's Hall")); in 1837 an order of the [Queen-in-Council](/wiki/Queen-in-Council "Queen-in-Council") was issued granting the university use of [Durham Castle](/wiki/Durham_Castle "Durham Castle"), which had previously been a palace of the bishop of Durham, for further accommodation.[[13]](#cite_note-Historic-13)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In 1846, [Bishop Hatfield](/wiki/Thomas_Hatfield "Thomas Hatfield")'s Hall (later to become [Hatfield College](/wiki/Hatfield_College "Hatfield College")) was founded, providing the opportunity for students to obtain affordable lodgings with fully catered communal eating, a revolutionary idea at the time, endorsed by a Royal Commission in 1862 and later spread to other universities. Those attending [University College](/wiki/University_College,_Durham "University College, Durham") were expected to bring a servant with them to deal with cooking, cleaning and so on. The level of applications to Bishop Hatfield's Hall led to a second hall along similar lines, [Bishop Cosin's Hall](/wiki/Bishop_Cosin%27s_Hall "Bishop Cosin's Hall"), being founded in 1851, although this only survived until 1864. Elsewhere, the university expanded from Durham into [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne") in 1852 when the medical school there (established in 1834) became a college of the university.[[13]](#cite_note-Historic-13) This was joined in 1871 by the College of Physical Sciences (renamed the College of Science in 1884 and again renamed Armstrong College in 1904). [St Cuthbert's Society](/wiki/St_Cuthbert%27s_Society "St Cuthbert's Society") was founded in 1888 for non-collegiate, mostly mature, male students as a non-residential society run by the students themselves. Two teacher-training colleges – St Hild's for women, established in 1858, and The College of the Venerable Bede for men, established in 1839,[[13]](#cite_note-Historic-13) also existed in the city and these merged to form the mixed [College of St Hild and St Bede](/wiki/College_of_St_Hild_and_St_Bede "College of St Hild and St Bede") in 1975. From 1896 these were associated with the university and graduates of St Hild's were the first female graduates from Durham in 1898.

During its expansion phase the university also became the first English university to establish relationships with overseas institutions;[[22]](#cite_note-22) firstly in 1875 with [Codrington College](/wiki/Codrington_College "Codrington College"), Barbados, and secondly in early 1876 with [Fourah Bay College](/wiki/Fourah_Bay_College "Fourah Bay College"), Sierra Leone.[[23]](#cite_note-Imperial-23) Under the arrangements the two colleges became affiliated colleges of the university with their students sitting examinations for and receiving Durham degrees.[[23]](#cite_note-Imperial-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) The landmark event was not met with universal applause, with the *[London Times](/wiki/London_Times "London Times")* stating "it would not be much longer before the University of Durham was affiliated to the Zoo".[[25]](#cite_note-25) After nearly a century of affiliation and with the prevailing winds of decolonisation, Fourah Bay became independent of the university in 1968 to form part of the [University of Sierra Leone](/wiki/University_of_Sierra_Leone "University of Sierra Leone")[[26]](#cite_note-26) while Codrington College became affiliated to the [University of the West Indies](/wiki/University_of_the_West_Indies "University of the West Indies") in 1965.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The first debating society in Durham was founded in 1835, but may have closed by 1839. The Durham University Union was established in 1842, and revived and moved to [Palace Green](/wiki/Palace_Green "Palace Green") in 1872–3 as the [Durham Union Society](/wiki/Durham_Union "Durham Union").[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) Notable past presidents of the Durham Union have included [Richard Dannatt](/wiki/Richard_Dannatt "Richard Dannatt"), [Sir Edward Leigh](/wiki/Sir_Edward_Leigh "Sir Edward Leigh"), and [Crispin Blunt](/wiki/Crispin_Blunt "Crispin Blunt").[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The Durham Colleges Students Representative Council (SRC) was founded around 1900 after the model of the College of Medicine SRC (in Newcastle). The Durham University SRC was formed in 1907 with representatives from the Durham Colleges, the College of Medicine, and Armstrong College (also in Newcastle). In 1963, after the creation of [Newcastle University](/wiki/Newcastle_University "Newcastle University"), the Durham Colleges SRC became the Durham University SRC, and was renamed as the [Durham Students' Union](/wiki/Durham_Students%27_Union "Durham Students' Union") in 1970.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Until the mid 19th century, University of Durham degrees were subject to a religion test and could only be taken by members of the established church. Medical degrees in Newcastle were exempt from this requirement from the start of the affiliation of the medical school, but in Durham it lasted until the revision of the statutes in 1865.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Despite the opening of degrees, staff and members of Convocation were still required to be members of the Church of England until the [Universities Tests Act 1871](/wiki/Universities_Tests_Act_1871 "Universities Tests Act 1871"). However, "dissenters" were able to attend Durham and then sit the examinations for degrees of the [University of London](/wiki/University_of_London "University of London"), which were not subject to any religious test.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Following the grant of a supplemental charter in 1895 allowing women to receive degrees of the university, the Women's Hostel ([St Mary's College](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_College,_Durham "St Mary's College, Durham") from 1919) was founded in 1899.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### The federal university, 1909–1963

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The federal university, 1909–1963")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Sutherland_Building_Newcastle_Tyne_11.jpg/250px-Sutherland_Building_Newcastle_Tyne_11.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sutherland_Building_Newcastle_Tyne_11.jpg)

Durham University College of Medicine, Newcastle, now the Sutherland Building of Northumbria University

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Durham_Heritage_Centre.jpg/250px-Durham_Heritage_Centre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_Heritage_Centre.jpg)

[St Chad's College](/wiki/St_Chad%27s_College "St Chad's College"), one of the two independent colleges

The Newcastle division of the university, which comprised both Armstrong College (named after Lord Armstrong) and Durham University College of Medicine, quickly grew to outnumber the Durham colleges, despite the addition of two independent [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") foundations: [St Chad's College](/wiki/St_Chad%27s_College "St Chad's College") (1904) and [St John's College](/wiki/St_John%27s_College,_Durham "St John's College, Durham") (1909). A parliamentary bill proposed in 1907 would have fixed the seat of the university in Durham for only ten years, allowing the Senate to choose to move to Newcastle after this. This was blocked by [John Taylor](/wiki/John_Wilkinson_Taylor_(politician) "John Wilkinson Taylor (politician)"), [MP](/wiki/Member_of_parliament "Member of parliament") for [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Chester-le-Street (UK Parliament constituency)"), with the support of graduates of the Durham colleges, until the bill was modified to establish a federal university with its seat fixed in Durham, as well as greater representation for the graduates (including that the chancellor would be appointed by convocation, as remains the case) and the abolition of *[ad eundem](/wiki/Ad_eundem "Ad eundem")* degrees.[[37]](#cite_note-37) This reform also removed the university from the authority of the Dean and Chapter of [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral"), who had been the governors of the university since its foundation.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Thirty years after this, the [Royal Commission](/wiki/Royal_Commission "Royal Commission") of 1937 recommended changes in the constitution of the federal university, resulting in the merger of the two colleges in the Newcastle Division to form King's College. The vice-chancellorship alternated between the warden of the Durham Division and the rector of the Newcastle Division, the chief academic and administrative officers of the two divisions under the 1937 statutes.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) The executive head of Durham University continues to use the title of "Vice-Chancellor and Warden".[[41]](#cite_note-41)

After World War II, the Durham division expanded rapidly. St Aidan's Society ([St Aidan's College](/wiki/St_Aidan%27s_College,_Durham "St Aidan's College, Durham") from 1961) was founded in 1947 to cater for non-resident women and the decision was made to expand further on Elvet Hill (where the science site had been established in the 1920s), relocating St Mary's College, building new men's colleges, vastly expanding the existing pure science provision in Durham, and adding applied science (1960) and engineering (1965).[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In 1947, the foundation stones for the new [St Mary's](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_College,_Durham "St Mary's College, Durham") College building on Elvet Hill were laid by Princess Elizabeth (later Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II")). The new building opened in 1952.[[43]](#cite_note-43) In the same year, tensions surfaced again over the Durham–Newcastle divide, with the university court putting forward a proposal to change the name of the university to the "University of Durham and Newcastle".[[44]](#cite_note-44) This motion was defeated in [convocation](/wiki/Convocation "Convocation") (the assembly of members of the university) by 135 votes to 129.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Eleven years later, with the [Universities of Durham and Newcastle upon Tyne Act 1963](/w/index.php?title=Universities_of_Durham_and_Newcastle_upon_Tyne_Act_1963&action=edit&redlink=1 "Universities of Durham and Newcastle upon Tyne Act 1963 (page does not exist)"), King's College became the [University of Newcastle upon Tyne](/wiki/Newcastle_University "Newcastle University"), leaving Durham University based solely in its home city.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### The modern university, 1963–1999

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: The modern university, 1963–1999")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/St_Mary%27s_College_Lawn.jpg/250px-St_Mary%27s_College_Lawn.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Mary%27s_College_Lawn.jpg)

The lawn at [St Mary's College](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_College,_Durham "St Mary's College, Durham"), the first of the [Hill colleges](/wiki/Colleges_of_the_University_of_Durham "Colleges of the University of Durham")

By the time of the separation from Newcastle the Elvet Hill site was well established; with the first of the new colleges being founded in 1959, [Grey College](/wiki/Grey_College,_Durham "Grey College, Durham"), named after the [second Earl Grey](/wiki/Charles_Grey,_2nd_Earl_Grey "Charles Grey, 2nd Earl Grey") who was the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") when the university was founded. Expansion up Elvet Hill continued, with [Van Mildert College](/wiki/Van_Mildert_College "Van Mildert College"), [Trevelyan College](/wiki/Trevelyan_College "Trevelyan College") (1966), and [Collingwood College](/wiki/Collingwood_College,_Durham "Collingwood College, Durham") (1972) all being added to the university, along with the relocation of the [botanic garden](/wiki/Durham_University_Botanic_Garden "Durham University Botanic Garden") (1970) and the [business school](/wiki/Durham_University_Business_School "Durham University Business School") (1977).[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)

These were not the only developments in the university, however. The Graduate Society, catering for postgraduate students, was founded in 1965 (renamed [Ustinov College](/wiki/Ustinov_College "Ustinov College") in 2003) and the Roman Catholic [seminary](/wiki/Seminary "Seminary") of [Ushaw College](/wiki/Ushaw_College "Ushaw College"), which had been in Durham since 1808, was licensed as a hall of residence in 1968 (closed 2011). In 1988 Hatfield, the last men's college, became mixed; followed by the women's college of Trevelyan in 1992, leaving the original women's college of St Mary's as the last single-sex college.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

In 1989 the university opened a fund-raising and [alumni](/wiki/Alumni "Alumni") office, with a virtual community for alumni.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

#### Development in Stockton, 1992–1999

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Development in Stockton, 1992–1999")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Ebsworth_Building%2C_Durham.jpg/250px-Ebsworth_Building%2C_Durham.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ebsworth_Building,_Durham.jpg)

Ebsworth Building, Queen's Campus, Stockton

In 1991, a joint venture between the university and the [University of Teesside](/wiki/University_of_Teesside "University of Teesside") saw the [Joint University College on Teesside of the Universities of Durham and Teesside](/wiki/Queen%27s_Campus,_Durham_University "Queen's Campus, Durham University") (JUCOT) established at [Thornaby-on-Tees](/wiki/Thornaby-on-Tees "Thornaby-on-Tees") in the borough of [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees "Stockton-on-Tees") and the ceremonial county of North Yorkshire, 30 miles (48 km) to the south of Durham. It opened under the name of University College Stockton (UCS) in 1992.[[53]](#cite_note-Mould-53)

UCS was initially intended to grant joint degrees validated by both institutions (BAs and BScs). However, Teesside, which had only become a university in 1992, had difficulties in taking on its responsibilities for the college and withdrew in 1994, Durham taking over full responsibility for UCS and the degrees to be awarded there.[[53]](#cite_note-Mould-53)

A programme of integration with Durham began, with the Privy Council approving changes in Durham's statutes to make UCS a college of the University of Durham. Further integration of the Stockton development with the university led to the formation of the University of Durham, Stockton Campus (UDSC) in 1998 and the separation of teaching responsibilities from UCS.[[53]](#cite_note-Mould-53)

### 21st century university

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 21st century university")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Al-Qasimi_Building.jpg/250px-Al-Qasimi_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Al-Qasimi_Building.jpg)

School of Government and International Affairs

In 2001, two new colleges, [John Snow](/wiki/John_Snow_College "John Snow College") and [George Stephenson](/wiki/Stephenson_College,_Durham "Stephenson College, Durham") (after the [physician](/wiki/John_Snow_(physician) "John Snow (physician)") and the [engineer](/wiki/George_Stephenson "George Stephenson")) were established at Stockton, replacing UCS, and the new [medical school](/wiki/Durham_University_School_of_Medicine,_Pharmacy_and_Health "Durham University School of Medicine, Pharmacy and Health") (operating in association with the [University of Newcastle upon Tyne](/wiki/University_of_Newcastle_upon_Tyne "University of Newcastle upon Tyne")) accepted its first students. In 2002, her [golden jubilee](/wiki/Golden_jubilee "Golden jubilee") year, the [queen](/wiki/Elizabeth_II_of_the_United_Kingdom "Elizabeth II of the United Kingdom") granted the title "Queen's Campus" to the Stockton site.[[54]](#cite_note-54) By 2005, Queen's Campus, Stockton, accounted for around 18 per cent of the total university student population.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

In 2005, the university unveiled a re-branded [logotype](/wiki/Logotype "Logotype") and introduced the trading name of Durham University, although the legal name of the institution remained the University of Durham and the official coat of arms was unchanged.[[7]](#cite_note-branding-7) The same year, [St Mary's College](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_College,_Durham "St Mary's College, Durham") had its first mixed undergraduate intake.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) In October 2006, [Josephine Butler College](/wiki/Josephine_Butler_College "Josephine Butler College") opened its doors to students as Durham's newest college – the first purpose-built self-catering college for students within Durham. This was the first new college to open in Durham itself since the creation of Collingwood in the 1970s.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

In May 2010, Durham joined the [Matariki Network of Universities](/wiki/Matariki_Network_of_Universities "Matariki Network of Universities") (MNU) together with [Dartmouth College](/wiki/Dartmouth_College "Dartmouth College") (US), [Queen's University](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_at_Kingston "Queen's University at Kingston") (Canada), [University of Otago](/wiki/University_of_Otago "University of Otago") (New Zealand), [University of Tübingen](/wiki/University_of_T%C3%BCbingen "University of Tübingen") (Germany), [University of Western Australia](/wiki/University_of_Western_Australia "University of Western Australia") and [Uppsala University](/wiki/Uppsala_University "Uppsala University") (Sweden).[[59]](#cite_note-59) In 2012, Durham (along with [York](/wiki/University_of_York "University of York"), [Exeter](/wiki/University_of_Exeter "University of Exeter") and [Queen Mary, University of London](/wiki/Queen_Mary,_University_of_London "Queen Mary, University of London")) joined the [Russell Group](/wiki/Russell_Group "Russell Group") of research-intensive British universities.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

Between 2010 and 2012 the university was criticised for accepting funds from controversial sources, including the government of Iran, the [US State Department](/wiki/US_State_Department "US State Department"), the prime minister of Kuwait, and [British American Tobacco](/wiki/British_American_Tobacco "British American Tobacco").[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)

#### Closure of Queen's Campus and expansion in Durham

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Closure of Queen's Campus and expansion in Durham")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Ogden_Centre_%28geograph_5908287%29.jpg/250px-Ogden_Centre_%28geograph_5908287%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ogden_Centre_(geograph_5908287).jpg)

The Ogden Centre for Fundamental Physics, next to the Department of Physics

The university announced in 2016 that it would relocate the colleges and academic activities currently at the Queen's Campus to Durham City from 2017;[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66) with the School of Medicine, Pharmacy and Health being transferred to [Newcastle University](/wiki/Newcastle_University "Newcastle University").[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) The Queen's Campus became an [International Study Centre](/wiki/Foundation_programme_(pre-undergraduate_courses)#International_foundation_years "Foundation programme (pre-undergraduate courses)") to prepare overseas students to study at Durham, run by [Study Group](/wiki/Study_Group_International "Study Group International").[[70]](#cite_note-Queen's_ISC-70)

In March 2017 [Lord Rees](/wiki/Martin_Rees "Martin Rees") opened the Ogden Centre for Fundamental Physics, designed by [Daniel Libeskind](/wiki/Daniel_Libeskind "Daniel Libeskind").[[71]](#cite_note-71) The new building, named after alumnus [Peter Ogden](/wiki/Peter_Ogden_(businessman) "Peter Ogden (businessman)"), provides extra laboratories and office space for 140 staff.[[72]](#cite_note-72) In May 2017 the university announced a new ten-year strategy that proposed investing £700m in improving the campus, creating 300 new academic posts, increasing the size of the university to 21,500 students while attracting more international students, and expanding the business school and the departments of law, politics, English and history to reach "critical research mass".[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-Chronicle_Strategy-74)

In 2018 the university announced that a consortium led by [Interserve](/wiki/Interserve "Interserve") would design, build and operate two colleges at [Mount Oswald](/wiki/Mount_Oswald "Mount Oswald") (new buildings for John Snow College and [one new college](/wiki/South_College,_Durham "South College, Durham")) for £105 million.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76) The project company (in which the university has a 15 per cent stake) financed the construction via a £90 million 46-year bond issue.[[77]](#cite_note-77) The two colleges opened in 2020.[[78]](#cite_note-78) Separately, the university announced that it had raised £225 million to fund its estate masterplan through the private sale of long-term bonds to British and US investors.[[79]](#cite_note-79) In 2021 it was reported that there was a culture of sexism and bullying at Durham, and that the university had been reluctant to address structural problems, thereby enabling this culture to develop relatively unchallenged.[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)

## Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Campus")]

Durham University owns a 257 ha (640-acre) estate[[6]](#cite_note-HESA_Estates-6) of which 251 ha (620 acres) is in Durham. This contains part of the Durham Castle and Cathedral [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site")[[83]](#cite_note-Durham's_World_Heritage_Site_expands-83) and multiple other heritage assets including three [ancient monuments](/wiki/Ancient_monument "Ancient monument") (the [Maiden Castle](/wiki/Maiden_Castle,_Durham "Maiden Castle, Durham") Iron Age [promontory fort](/wiki/Promontory_fort "Promontory fort"),[[84]](#cite_note-84) [Cosin's Library](/wiki/Cosin%27s_Library "Cosin's Library")[[85]](#cite_note-85) and [Divinity House](/wiki/Durham_University_Department_of_Music#Buildings "Durham University Department of Music")[[86]](#cite_note-86)), four [grade I listed buildings](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building") (including [Kingsgate Bridge](/wiki/Kingsgate_Bridge "Kingsgate Bridge"),[[87]](#cite_note-87) the Exchequer Building on [Palace Green](/wiki/Palace_Green "Palace Green"),[[88]](#cite_note-88) the gatehouse,[[89]](#cite_note-89) keep,[[90]](#cite_note-90) north range[[91]](#cite_note-91) and west range[[92]](#cite_note-92) of [Durham Castle](/wiki/Durham_Castle "Durham Castle"), and multiple listings covering surviving sections of the castle walls around the north of the castle[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95) and along the top of the river bank behind Hatfield College[[96]](#cite_note-96) and St Cuthbert's Society[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)) and 79 grade II or II\* listed buildings.[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-Durham_Masterplan-100) As of 2023,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit) the estate
in Durham included 112 ha (280 acres) of woodland scrub (with 46 ha (110 acres) of woodland designated as Areas of High Landscape Value, including the 32.4 ha (80 acres) of Great High Wood, Hollingside Wood and Blaid's Wood additionally designated as [Ancient Semi-Natural Woodlands](/wiki/Ancient_woodland "Ancient woodland"), [Sites of Nature Conservation Importance](/wiki/Site_of_Nature_Conservation_Interest "Site of Nature Conservation Interest") and Sites of Ecological Value[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102)), 53 ha (130 acres) of farming and grazing land, and 27 ha (67 acres) of amenity grassland, alongside 51.4 ha (127 acres) of built environment.[[103]](#cite_note-Biodiversity-103) The estate also includes the [Queen's Campus](/wiki/Queen%27s_Campus,_Durham_University "Queen's Campus, Durham University") in [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Borough_of_Stockton-on-Tees "Borough of Stockton-on-Tees").

One of the major public attractions in Durham City is the 10 ha (25-acre) [Botanic Gardens](/wiki/Durham_University_Botanic_Garden "Durham University Botanic Garden"), established (on the current site) in 1970, with over 80,000 visitors annually.[[104]](#cite_note-About_the_Garden-104) As of 2021,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit) the university estate contains over 380 buildings with a floor area of 424,600 square metres (4,570,000 sq ft), including 189,400 square metres (2,039,000 sq ft) of residential area in 170 residential buildings (not including the independent St Chad's and St John's colleges, which are not owned by the university).[[6]](#cite_note-HESA_Estates-6) The insurance reinstatement value was estimated as close to £850 million in 2014.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

### Durham City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Durham City")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Hatfield_College%2C_Durham.jpg/250px-Hatfield_College%2C_Durham.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hatfield_College,_Durham.jpg)

[Hatfield College](/wiki/Hatfield_College,_Durham "Hatfield College, Durham"), one of the five colleges along the Bailey

Durham City is the main location of the university and contains all of the colleges and academic departments. The Durham City estate is spread across several different sites.

#### The Peninsula

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: The Peninsula")]

Further information: [The Bailey](/wiki/The_Bailey "The Bailey") and [Palace Green](/wiki/Palace_Green "Palace Green")

[The Bailey](/wiki/The_Bailey "The Bailey") and [Palace Green](/wiki/Palace_Green "Palace Green") form the historic centre of the university and contain five colleges as well as the departments of music, history, classics and ancient history, and theology and religion, the Institute of Advanced Study, [Palace Green Library](/wiki/Palace_Green_Library "Palace Green Library") (housing the university's special collections), the [archaeological museum](/wiki/Durham_University_Museum_of_Archaeology "Durham University Museum of Archaeology"), the [Durham Union Society](/wiki/Durham_Union_Society "Durham Union Society"), and the [Assembly Rooms Theatre](/wiki/Assembly_Rooms_Theatre_(Durham) "Assembly Rooms Theatre (Durham)"). The Bailey is linked to [Dunelm House](/wiki/Dunelm_House "Dunelm House"), home of [Durham Students' Union](/wiki/Durham_Students%27_Union "Durham Students' Union") in New Elvet, by the university's [Kingsgate Bridge](/wiki/Kingsgate_Bridge "Kingsgate Bridge").[[106]](#cite_note-location-106)

#### Elvet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Elvet")]

Further information: [Elvet](/wiki/Elvet "Elvet")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Old_Elvet%2C_Durham_%28geograph_3851375%29.jpg/250px-Old_Elvet%2C_Durham_%28geograph_3851375%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Elvet,_Durham_(geograph_3851375).jpg)

[Old Elvet](/wiki/Old_Elvet "Old Elvet") is the home of a number of departments

The [Elvet](/wiki/Elvet "Elvet") area contains the departments of English studies and philosophy along with the school of modern languages and cultures in the faculty of arts as well as department of sport and exercise sciences of the faculty of social sciences and health. While many of the departments are in converted houses, the Elvet Riverside complex is home to the school of modern languages and cultures and parts of the department of English studies. Durham Students' Union is based in Dunelm House, just north of Elvet Riverside, linked to the peninsula area by Kingsgate Bridge. [The Racecourse](/wiki/The_Racecourse "The Racecourse") has university playing fields, including the main cricket ground. While no colleges are based in the Elvet area, it does contain the Parson's Field accommodation for St Cuthbert's Society and Hatfield College's James Barber House and Palmer's Garth accommodation.[[106]](#cite_note-location-106)

#### Leazes Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Leazes Road")]

The Leazes Road site on the north bank of the Wear, opposite the Racecourse playing fields and Old Elvet, was home to the School of Education and Hild Bede College. As of 2024,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit) this site is being redeveloped and refurbished with the aim that a 19th college will be established adjacent to Hild Bede. In preparation for redevelopment, departments and facilities were relocated from Leazes Road in 2022,[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-Education_relocation-109) and Hild Bede college moved temporarily to Rushford Court in the viaduct area in summer 2024. [UPP](/wiki/University_Partnerships_Programme "University Partnerships Programme") were announced in May 2024 as the preferred bidders to deliver the refurbishment of Hild Bede and the building of the new 19th college under a [design, build, fund and operate](/wiki/Design_build_finance_and_operate "Design build finance and operate") model.[[110]](#cite_note-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111)

#### Mountjoy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Mountjoy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Palatine_Centre%2C_Durham_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Palatine_Centre%2C_Durham_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palatine_Centre,_Durham_(cropped).jpg)

The Palatine Centre on the Mountjoy site, home of the university's administration

Further information: [Mountjoy, Durham](/wiki/Mountjoy,_Durham "Mountjoy, Durham")

The Mountjoy site (formerly the Science site) south of Elvet contains many of the university's departments, including all of the departments in the faculty of science and most of the departments in the faculty of social science and health. It is also home to the university's central administration, in the Palatine Centre, the main Bill Bryson Library, and central teaching facilities at the Calman Learning Centre and the [Lower Mountjoy Teaching and Learning Centre](/wiki/Lower_Mountjoy_Teaching_and_Learning_Centre "Lower Mountjoy Teaching and Learning Centre").[[106]](#cite_note-location-106)

#### Elvet Hill

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Elvet Hill")]

Further information: [Colleges of Durham University](/wiki/Colleges_of_Durham_University "Colleges of Durham University")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/South_and_Snow_Colleges_Durham_-_wide_view.jpg/250px-South_and_Snow_Colleges_Durham_-_wide_view.jpg)](/wiki/File:South_and_Snow_Colleges_Durham_-_wide_view.jpg)

South (left) and John Snow (right) colleges on the Mount Oswald site

Elvet Hill, south of the Mountjoy site, has ten of the colleges as well as the [botanic garden](/wiki/Durham_University_Botanic_Garden "Durham University Botanic Garden") and the vice-chancellor's residence in Hollingside House. Mill Hill Lane is home to the departments of accounting, economics and finance from the [business school](/wiki/Durham_University_Business_School "Durham University Business School"),[[112]](#cite_note-112) and the department of sociology from the faculty of social sciences,[[113]](#cite_note-113) Elsewhere on Elvet Hill are the school of government and international affairs from the faculty of social sciences in the Al-Qasimi Building, as well as the [Teikyo University of Japan in Durham](/wiki/Teikyo_University_of_Japan_in_Durham "Teikyo University of Japan in Durham") and [Durham University Oriental Museum](/wiki/Durham_University_Oriental_Museum "Durham University Oriental Museum").[[106]](#cite_note-location-106)

#### Elsewhere in Durham

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Elsewhere in Durham")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Sheraton_Park_Durham.jpg/250px-Sheraton_Park_Durham.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheraton_Park_Durham.jpg)

Sheraton Park, site of a former teacher training college and home to Ustinov College since 2017

Ustinov College, Durham's postgraduate college, is located in Sheraton Park, beyond Elvet Hill.[[114]](#cite_note-114) [Rushford Court](/wiki/Rushford_Court "Rushford Court"), owned by [Unite Students](/wiki/Unite_Students "Unite Students"), is the temporary home of Hild Bede College as of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit) and was also used by John Snow College in 2019–20. In the longer term, it is planned to become Durham's 18th college.[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116)

The Waterside Building, home of the [business school](/wiki/Durham_University_Business_School "Durham University Business School")'s Department of Management and Marketing from 2024, is on the east bank of the River Wear north (downstream) of the city centre, in the Durham Innovation District.[[117]](#cite_note-117)[[118]](#cite_note-118) Plans were approved in 2025 for a new university [data centre](/wiki/Data_centre "Data centre") at Aykley Heads, also within the innovation district, to house a new supercomputer.[[119]](#cite_note-119) Boldon House in [Pity Me](/wiki/Pity_Me "Pity Me") houses professional services staff.[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121)

#### Development plans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Development plans")]

The university published a strategy document in 2017 setting out (among other things) a roadmap for development of the estate over the period to 2027, including the development of a new home for the business school at Elvet Waterside (Old Elvet), planned to open in 2021, the redevelopment of the arts and humanities facilities at Elvet Riverside (New Elvet), planned to open from 2022, the construction of four to six new colleges, and the continued development of the Mountjoy site.[[122]](#cite_note-Roadmap_2027-122)[[123]](#cite_note-123) The university's *Estate Masterplan* for 2017–2027 identified the area around Howlands Farm (Josephine Butler and Stephenson colleges), the Leazes Road site (Hild Bede College), and the business school site on Mill Hill as possible locations for new accommodation development (i.e., new colleges).[[124]](#cite_note-Colleges_Masterplan-124) A 'strategy refresh' was approved in 2023, which included establishing a new cultural quarter on the old swimming baths site in Elvet and developing interdisciplinary science research and laboratory facilities at Upper Mountjoy.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

In February 2025, the university requested an Environmental Impact Assessment Scoping Opinion from the county council regarding plans for work on the Hild-Bede site that would involve the demolition of multiple buildings, including the non-designated heritage asset Haworth House, and construction of the new 19th college and a hub building, with an estimated 375 bed spaces in the retained buildings and 600 in the new buildings. The request also included redevelopment of postgraduate accommodation on Leazes Road, with 30 refurbished bed spaces and 70 new spaces, and the development of new postgraduate accommodation with 450 bed spaces on the site of Elvet Hill car park.[[126]](#cite_note-126)[[127]](#cite_note-127) The university clarified in March 2025 that these were "options being explored" and might not form part of the final plan.[[128]](#cite_note-128) In March 2026, the university announced that the redevelopment with the preferred external bidder had been halted due to "the very challenging cost and nature of construction projects at the moment".[[129]](#cite_note-129)

#### Ushaw College

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Ushaw College")]

Main article: [Ushaw College](/wiki/Ushaw_College "Ushaw College")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Ushaw_Georgian_house_main_building.jpg/250px-Ushaw_Georgian_house_main_building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ushaw_Georgian_house_main_building.jpg)

Ushaw College main building

[Ushaw College](/wiki/Ushaw_College "Ushaw College"), five miles west of Durham, is a former Catholic seminary that was a licensed hall of residence of the university. It hosts parts of the business school and of the centre for Catholic studies, with the university having committed to leasing the east wing until 2027 and to establishing a residential research library at Ushaw.[[130]](#cite_note-Ushaw_Library-130)

### Queen's Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Queen's Campus")]

Main article: [Queen's Campus, Durham University](/wiki/Queen%27s_Campus,_Durham_University "Queen's Campus, Durham University")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Wolfson_Institute.jpg/500px-Wolfson_Institute.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wolfson_Institute.jpg)

[Wolfson Research Institute](/wiki/Wolfson_Research_Institute "Wolfson Research Institute") at the Queen's Campus

Queen's Campus is in the [borough of Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Borough_of_Stockton-on-Tees "Borough of Stockton-on-Tees") ([Thornaby](/wiki/Thornaby "Thornaby"), [North Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Yorkshire "North Yorkshire")) 30 miles from Durham City. Until 2017–18, the campus was home to around 2,000 full-time students in two colleges ([John Snow](/wiki/John_Snow_College "John Snow College") and [Stephenson Colleges](/wiki/Stephenson_College,_Durham "Stephenson College, Durham")) and the [Wolfson Research Institute](/wiki/Wolfson_Research_Institute "Wolfson Research Institute"),[[131]](#cite_note-Welcome_to_Queen's_-_A_Cosmopolitan_Campus-131) with a bus connecting Queen's Campus to Durham City.[[132]](#cite_note-Stockton-132)

The colleges and academic departments were relocated between 2017 and 2018, and Queen's Campus became an International Study Centre (ISC) run by [Study Group](/wiki/Study_Group_International "Study Group International"), preparing non-EU foreign students to enter degree courses at the university, from September 2017.[[70]](#cite_note-Queen's_ISC-70)[[122]](#cite_note-Roadmap_2027-122) The former John Snow College buildings were sold in 2020[[133]](#cite_note-133) and, from 2022, only the Ebsworth building has been used by the ISC.[[134]](#cite_note-134)

### Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Libraries")]

Main article: [Durham University Library](/wiki/Durham_University_Library "Durham University Library")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Palace_Green_Library_%28cropped%29.jpg/500px-Palace_Green_Library_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palace_Green_Library_(cropped).jpg)

The original university library, now known as the [Palace Green](/wiki/Palace_Green "Palace Green") Library (centre), and the [School of Music](/wiki/Durham_University_Department_of_Music "Durham University Department of Music") (left)

[Durham University Library](/wiki/Durham_University_Library "Durham University Library") was founded in January 1833 at [Palace Green](/wiki/Palace_Green "Palace Green") with a 160-volume donation by the bishop of Durham, William Van Mildert, and now holds over 1.5 million printed items across four branches: Bill Bryson Library (the main library), Queen's Campus Library, Durham University Business School Library and Palace Green Library, which holds the special and heritage collections.[[135]](#cite_note-Library_History-135)[[136]](#cite_note-136)

In 2005, designated status was granted by the [Museums, Libraries and Archives Council](/wiki/Museums,_Libraries_and_Archives_Council "Museums, Libraries and Archives Council") to two of the special collections: [Bishop Cosin's Library](/wiki/Cosin%27s_Library "Cosin's Library") on Palace Green (founded in 1669 and including medieval manuscripts and early printed books) and the Sudan Archive, held in Palace Green Library.[[137]](#cite_note-Library:_Designation-137)[[138]](#cite_note-138)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/St_Cuthbert_Gospel_-_f.1.jpg/250px-St_Cuthbert_Gospel_-_f.1.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Cuthbert_Gospel_-_f.1.jpg)

St Cuthbert Gospel, an 8th-century [gospel book](/wiki/Gospel_book "Gospel book")

In 2012 the university, together with the [British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library") and [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral"), purchased Europe's oldest intact book, the [St Cuthbert Gospel](/wiki/St_Cuthbert_Gospel "St Cuthbert Gospel"), for the nation for £9 million. It is displayed equally in London and Durham, being shown at the university's Palace Green Library for the first time as part of the Lindisfarne Gospels Durham exhibition in 2013.[[139]](#cite_note-139)[[140]](#cite_note-140)[[141]](#cite_note-141)

In addition to the central library system, many colleges maintain their own libraries,[[142]](#cite_note-142) such as the Bettenson, Brewis, Williams and Fenton Libraries of St Chad's College, which contain over 38,000 volumes.[[143]](#cite_note-St_Chad's:_Library-143) Durham staff and students can also access the libraries of [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral") and [Ushaw College](/wiki/Ushaw_College "Ushaw College").[[144]](#cite_note-144) Some departments, such as classics and ancient history, also have their own libraries.[[145]](#cite_note-145)

In February 2017, the university announced a £2 million investment to establish the first residential research library at a UK university in collaboration with [Ushaw College](/wiki/Ushaw_College "Ushaw College") and [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral") and offering access to the collections of all three institutions.[[130]](#cite_note-Ushaw_Library-130)

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Museums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Durham_Kathedrale.jpg/250px-Durham_Kathedrale.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_Kathedrale.jpg)

The Old Fulling Mill, original home of the Durham University Museum in 1833, on the bank of the River Wear below Durham Cathedral

The university's Museums, Galleries and Exhibitions manages three museums open to the public, all accredited by [Arts Council England](/wiki/Arts_Council_England "Arts Council England") through the UK Museum Accreditation Scheme, as well as two non-public collections. Total holdings are over 100,000 pieces.[[146]](#cite_note-146)[[147]](#cite_note-147)

Built in the 1960s, the [Durham University Oriental Museum](/wiki/Durham_University_Oriental_Museum "Durham University Oriental Museum") grew predominantly from the acquisitions of the university's former School of Oriental Studies.[[148]](#cite_note-Oriental_Museum:History_of_the_Museum-148) The collection contains over 30,000 objects from Asian art to antiquities, covering the [Orient](/wiki/Orient "Orient") and [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") to the Far East and the [Indian Sub-continent](/wiki/Indian_Sub-continent "Indian Sub-continent"), with over a third of the collection relating to China.[[148]](#cite_note-Oriental_Museum:History_of_the_Museum-148)[[149]](#cite_note-Oriental_Museum:Chinese-149) The Chinese collection and the Egyptian collection were granted [Designated Status](/wiki/Designation_Scheme "Designation Scheme") as collections of national importance by the [Museums, Libraries and Archives Council](/wiki/Museums,_Libraries_and_Archives_Council "Museums, Libraries and Archives Council") in 2008.[[149]](#cite_note-Oriental_Museum:Chinese-149)[[150]](#cite_note-Oriental_Museum:Egyptian-150)[[151]](#cite_note-DESIGNATION_UPDATE_Spring_2008-151)

The [Durham University Museum of Archaeology](/wiki/Durham_University_Museum_of_Archaeology "Durham University Museum of Archaeology"), housed in Palace Green Library, originally opened in 1833 and was the second university museum in England to allow admittance to the general public.[[152]](#cite_note-Museum_of_Archaeology:History_of_the_Museum-152) It focuses on the heritage of North East England and includes national and international collections spanning the [Prehistoric](/wiki/Prehistoric "Prehistoric"), Ancient Greek, [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain"), [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon "Anglo-Saxon"), [Medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") and [Post Medieval](/wiki/Post_Medieval "Post Medieval") periods.[[153]](#cite_note-Museum_of_Archaeology:Collections_of_the_Museum-153)

[Durham Castle Museum](/wiki/Durham_Castle "Durham Castle") has around 5,000 pieces from the history of the castle, including suits of armour, tapestries, silverware and art.[[154]](#cite_note-154)

In addition to the three public museums, the university also holds a biosciences collection[[155]](#cite_note-155) and an art collection.[[156]](#cite_note-156)

### Chapels, prayer rooms and other faith resources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Chapels, prayer rooms and other faith resources")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/The_Norman_Chapel_of_Durham_Castle.jpg/250px-The_Norman_Chapel_of_Durham_Castle.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Norman_Chapel_of_Durham_Castle.jpg)

University College's Norman Chapel, built c. 1080, is the oldest surviving building in Durham[[157]](#cite_note-Norman_Chapel-157)

There are Anglican chapels at many of the colleges, including the 11th century Norman Chapel in University College[[157]](#cite_note-Norman_Chapel-157) and the art deco chapel in Hild Bede College.[[158]](#cite_note-158)
There are also multi-faith rooms at St Aidan's College,[[159]](#cite_note-St_Aidan's_faith_support-159) Trevelyan College,[[160]](#cite_note-160) and in the hub building shared by John Snow and South colleges.[[161]](#cite_note-161) Muslim prayer rooms are located in Old Elvet and at Grey College.[[162]](#cite_note-162) There is a kosher kitchen in St Aidan's College which supports Jewish Sabbath meals and other festivals.[[159]](#cite_note-St_Aidan's_faith_support-159)

### Environmental initiatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Environmental initiatives")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Footpath_in_Great_High_Wood_Durham_City_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2354182.jpg/250px-Footpath_in_Great_High_Wood_Durham_City_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2354182.jpg)](/wiki/File:Footpath_in_Great_High_Wood_Durham_City_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2354182.jpg)

A path through the Great High Wood, an [ancient woodland](/wiki/Ancient_woodland "Ancient woodland") on the university campus

Durham has committed to reaching [Net Zero](/wiki/Net_Zero "Net Zero") by 2035 and to achieving a net [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity") gain on campus by 2032.[[163]](#cite_note-163) The university joined the Hedgehog Friendly Campus campaign in 2020, receiving silver accreditation in 2021/22 and gold accreditation in 2022/23,[[164]](#cite_note-164) before becoming one of only six universities with the highest level platinum accreditation in 2024.[[165]](#cite_note-platinum-165) It was a founding member of the Nature Positive Universities Alliance in 2022.[[166]](#cite_note-166) Actions taken to enhance biodiversity have included converting 9 hectares (22 acres) of lawns to grassland and enriching 4 hectares (10 acres) of land in the botanic garden to increase wildflower diversity.[[165]](#cite_note-platinum-165)

The green initiatives at the university and its rise in two years from 96th (3rd class) to 30th in the *[People and Planet](/wiki/People_and_Planet "People and Planet") University League* (between 2019 and 2021) have been noted in the national press.[[167]](#cite_note-167) In the 2024/25 table, it was ranked 48th (2:1 class).[[168]](#cite_note-168) Internationally, the university was ranked joint 24th globally for sustainability in the *[QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings"): Sustainability 2026*[[169]](#cite_note-169) and 34th globally in the 2025 *[Times Higher Education](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education "Times Higher Education") Impact Rankings*, based on the UN [Sustainable Development Goals](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goals "Sustainable Development Goals").[[170]](#cite_note-170)

Durham has won multiple [Green Gown Awards](/wiki/Green_Gown_Awards "Green Gown Awards"):

* Nature Positive category (2023) for the Enhancing Biodiversity plan[[171]](#cite_note-171) (also 'highly commended' at the International Green Gown Awards 2024)[[172]](#cite_note-172)
* Enterprise category (2021) for Project RENU (Research Expedition for Net zero and Universal learning)[[173]](#cite_note-173)
* Community category (2017) for Van Mildert College Outreach[[174]](#cite_note-174)
* Continuous Improvement category (2015) for the Greenspace Movement[[175]](#cite_note-175)
* Sustainable Procurement category (2011)[[176]](#cite_note-176)
* Sustainable Procurement category (2010)[[177]](#cite_note-177)

Durham University established the MammalWeb [citizen science](/wiki/Citizen_science "Citizen science") platform in 2013 in partnership with [Durham Wildlife Trust](/wiki/Durham_Wildlife_Trust "Durham Wildlife Trust"). This is now a national non-profit and remains closely linked to the university. The project won the [National Biodiversity Network](/wiki/National_Biodiversity_Network "National Biodiversity Network") Group Award in 2024.[[178]](#cite_note-178)[[179]](#cite_note-179)

In 2025, the installed [solar power](/wiki/Solar_power "Solar power") capacity on Durham University buildings reached 1 MW, expected to provide just under 1 GWh of electricity per year.[[180]](#cite_note-180)

## Organisation and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Organisation and administration")]

### Academic year

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Academic year")]

The academic year at Durham is divided into three terms: [Michaelmas term](/wiki/Michaelmas_term "Michaelmas term"), which lasts 10 weeks from October to December; [Epiphany term](/wiki/Epiphany_term "Epiphany term"), which lasts ten weeks from January to March; and [Easter term](/wiki/Easter_term "Easter term"), which lasts nine weeks from April to June. All terms start on a Monday. The weeks of term are called "Teaching Weeks", numbered from 1 (start of Michaelmas) to 29 (end of Easter), although this period is used for teaching and exams. Additionally, there is an "Induction Week" (informally known as "[Freshers' Week](/wiki/Student_orientation "Student orientation")" or Week 0) for first year students prior to the start of Michaelmas term, starting on the first Monday in October.[[181]](#cite_note-181)

Main article: [Colleges of Durham University](/wiki/Colleges_of_Durham_University "Colleges of Durham University")

Durham operates a collegiate structure with the colleges being "listed bodies" under the [Education Reform Act 1988](/wiki/Education_Reform_Act_1988 "Education Reform Act 1988"),[[182]](#cite_note-182) as bodies that appear to the Secretary of State "to be a constituent college, school, hall or other institution of a university which is such a recognised body" (the "recognised body" being, in this case, Durham University).[[183]](#cite_note-183) Though most of the Durham colleges are governed and owned directly by the university itself (the exceptions being St John's and St Chad's), the legal status of the Durham colleges as listed bodies is similar to those at [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") and [Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"), setting them apart from those at the universities of Lancaster and York.[[184]](#cite_note-184) However, unlike at Oxford and Cambridge, there is no formal teaching at Durham colleges (with the exception of [Cranmer Hall](/wiki/Cranmer_Hall,_Durham "Cranmer Hall, Durham") theological college within St John's), although colleges are active in research.[[185]](#cite_note-185)[[186]](#cite_note-186)[[187]](#cite_note-187) All of the college heads are *ex officio* members of the university [senate](/wiki/Academic_senate "Academic senate").[[188]](#cite_note-Statutes-188): statute 4

[Formal dinners](/wiki/Formal_(university) "Formal (university)") (known as "formals") are held at every college; [gowns](/wiki/Academic_dress_of_Durham_University "Academic dress of Durham University") are worn to these events at just over half of the colleges.[[189]](#cite_note-189)[[190]](#cite_note-190) There is a great deal of intercollegiate rivalry, particularly in [rowing](/wiki/Durham_College_Rowing "Durham College Rowing") and other sporting activities. There is also rivalry between the older "[Bailey](/wiki/The_Bailey "The Bailey")" colleges and the newer "Hill" colleges on Elvet Hill.[[191]](#cite_note-191)[[192]](#cite_note-192)

### Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Governance")]

See also: [List of Chancellors of the University of Durham](/wiki/List_of_Chancellors_of_the_University_of_Durham "List of Chancellors of the University of Durham") and [List of Vice-Chancellor & Wardens of the University of Durham](/wiki/List_of_Vice-Chancellor_%26_Wardens_of_the_University_of_Durham "List of Vice-Chancellor & Wardens of the University of Durham")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/The_Venerable_Charles_Thorp.jpg/250px-The_Venerable_Charles_Thorp.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Venerable_Charles_Thorp.jpg)

Archdeacon [Charles Thorp](/wiki/Charles_Thorp "Charles Thorp"), founder and first Warden of Durham

The university is governed by the statutes put in place by the Universities of Durham and Newcastle upon Tyne Act, 1963 and subsequently amended by council and approved by the [King in Council](/wiki/King_in_Council "King in Council"), most recently in 2023, and by [ordinances](/wiki/Ordinance_(university) "Ordinance (university)") passed by council.[[188]](#cite_note-Statutes-188)

[Council](/wiki/University_council "University council") is the governing body, the corporation and the charity trustees of the university. It is composed of eleven internal and thirteen external ('lay') members. There are three ex-officio members internal to the university: the vice-chancellor and warden, the deputy vice-chancellor and provost, and the president of Durham Student's Union, along with seven appointed members from the university, who may not be members of the university executive committee and five of whom must be members of academic staff, and one appointed postgraduate student nominated by the student's union. There are also twelve appointed lay members (not being teachers or salaried staff in the university or any of its colleges), and the [Dean of Durham](/wiki/Dean_of_Durham "Dean of Durham") as an ex-officio lay member.[[188]](#cite_note-Statutes-188): statute 3 The chair of the council, the deputy chair, and the senior independent governor must be lay members of the council.[[193]](#cite_note-Council-193) Since August 2025, the chair of council has been Caroline Johnstone.[[194]](#cite_note-194) While there is no formal requirement for alumni representation on the council, six of the appointment lay members (including the deputy chair) were Durham graduates as of the 2024/25 academic year.[[195]](#cite_note-195)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Bill_Bryson_Chancellor.JPG/250px-Bill_Bryson_Chancellor.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bill_Bryson_Chancellor.JPG)

[Bill Bryson](/wiki/Bill_Bryson "Bill Bryson") (Chancellor 2005–2012) in the academic dress of Chancellor of Durham University

The [chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_(education) "Chancellor (education)") is the ceremonial head of the university and one of the statutory officers.[[188]](#cite_note-Statutes-188): statute 7[[196]](#cite_note-Officers-196) The current chancelor is [Fiona Hill](/wiki/Fiona_Hill_(presidential_advisor) "Fiona Hill (presidential advisor)"), who was appointed in November 2022 in succession to [Sir Thomas Allen](/wiki/Thomas_Allen_(baritone) "Thomas Allen (baritone)") and took office in June 2023.[[197]](#cite_note-197)[[198]](#cite_note-198) The chancellor is appointed by convocation for "a term of up to five years" with "no scope for re-appointment".[[196]](#cite_note-Officers-196) The role of the chancellor is ceremonial, being to confer degrees and to act as an ambassador for the university; the [vice-chancellor and warden](/wiki/Vice-chancellor "Vice-chancellor") is the [chief executive officer](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") of the university and is appointed by council after consultation with senate.[[188]](#cite_note-Statutes-188): statute 8[[196]](#cite_note-Officers-196) As warden, the vice-chancellor is responsible for the 15 maintained colleges of the university.[[199]](#cite_note-199) Since January 2022 this has been [Karen O'Brien](/wiki/Karen_O%27Brien "Karen O'Brien"), the university's first female vice-chancellor and warden, succeeding [Stuart Corbridge](/wiki/Stuart_Corbridge "Stuart Corbridge") who retired in July 2021.[[200]](#cite_note-200) The other statutory officers of the university are the [university secretary](/wiki/Company_secretary "Company secretary")[[188]](#cite_note-Statutes-188): statute 9 and the [chief financial officer](/wiki/Chief_financial_officer "Chief financial officer"),[[188]](#cite_note-Statutes-188): statute 10 while other (non-statutory) officers established by council are the [deputy vice-chancellor and provost](/wiki/Provost_(education) "Provost (education)"), the [pro-vice-chancellors](/wiki/Pro-vice-chancellor "Pro-vice-chancellor"), the [executive deans](/wiki/Dean_(education) "Dean (education)") of the faculties, and the [chief operating officer](/wiki/Chief_operating_officer "Chief operating officer").[[196]](#cite_note-Officers-196)

The day-to-day running of the university is in the hands of the University Executive Committee, which is established by the ordinances as a joint committee of council and senate. It supports the vice-chancellor and warden in their statutory responsibility for the performance and strategic direction of the university and is chaired by the vice-chancellor and warden.[[201]](#cite_note-UEC-201)

[Convocation](/wiki/Convocation "Convocation") is the assembly of the university, with the purpose of discussing the latest developments and future plans of the university, receiving the university's annual report, and appointing the chancellor. Membership of convocation includes the chancellor, vice-chancellor and warden, deputy vice-chancellor provost, pro-vice-chancellors, the alumni and alumnae of the university, and other officers appointed to convocation by council on the recommendation of senate. It meets at least once a year to receive the annual report and further extraordinary meetings can be called if representation is made by a minimum of 50 members or if called by the vice-chancellor and warden. Appointment of the chancellor is on the joint nomination of council and senate, and is by majority vote of the members of convocation present and voting.[[188]](#cite_note-Statutes-188): statute 16[[196]](#cite_note-Officers-196)[[202]](#cite_note-202)

The [visitor](/wiki/Visitor "Visitor") of the university is the [bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham"), or a suffragan bishop if the see of Durham is vacant.[[188]](#cite_note-Statutes-188): statutes 2 and 6 The visitor's power is limited to matters internal to the university, where they may settle disputes "relating to the interpretation and application of the university's statues" and "between members of the university in their capacity as members". The visitor may also "inspect and regulate members' actions and behaviour in their capacities as members" and "correct abuses and irregularities in the internal regulation of the university". Following the [Higher Education Act 2004](/wiki/Higher_Education_Act_2004 "Higher Education Act 2004"), the visitor has no jurisdiction regarding student complaints or employment disputes between staff and the university.[[203]](#cite_note-203)

#### Academic governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Academic governance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Durham_Graduation.jpg/250px-Durham_Graduation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_Graduation.jpg)

The university's graduation ceremonies, known as [congregations](/wiki/Congregation_(university) "Congregation (university)"), take place in [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral") with 'congregation fair' receptions on [Palace Green](/wiki/Palace_Green "Palace Green")[[204]](#cite_note-204)

Academic governance is overseen by the [senate](/wiki/Academic_Senate "Academic Senate"), with council having the responsibility of "receiv[ing] and test[ing] assurances that [this] is adequate and effective".[[193]](#cite_note-Council-193) Senate is, along with council, one of the two statutory bodies of the university,[[188]](#cite_note-Statutes-188): statute 2 and is established by the university statutes, with membership including the vice-chancellor and warden, the deputy vice-chancellor and provost, the pro-vice-chancellors, faculty deans, heads of departments, heads of colleges, elected representatives of the academic staff, and student representatives. It operates under the delegated authority of the council and has the statutory duty to "inform and provide assurance to the Council regarding the University's academic standards, quality of education and research".[[188]](#cite_note-Statutes-188): statute 4 It has the power to grant and revoke degrees (including honorary degrees) and also regulates admissions, examinations, and the use of the [academic dress of the university](/wiki/Academic_dress_of_Durham_University "Academic dress of Durham University"), as well as having oversight of student discipline and student experience.[[205]](#cite_note-Senate-205)

The academic electoral assembly (AEA) is established by the statutes of the university and consists of all members of academic staff, except those who are ex-officio members of senate, and the vice-principals of the recognised colleges and any other members of staff performing academic or academic-related roles. It elects the academic staff representatives to senate; these also constitute the standing committee of the AEA. The standing committee elects the chair and vice-chair of the AEA from among the members elected to senate.
In addition to its role in electing members of senate, the AEA provides a forum for staff "to inform matters of academic governance and provide comment to the Senate".[[188]](#cite_note-Statutes-188): statute 13[[206]](#cite_note-AEA-206) The statutes also allow for meetings of all members of the academic staff to be called by the vice-chancellor and warden, which may discuss any matter of interest to the university and make representations to the statutory bodies (council and senate). If requested in writing by one hundred or more members of the academic staff, the vice-chancellor and warden must call and attend a meeting of the academic staff.[[188]](#cite_note-Statutes-188): statute 18

#### Trade unions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Trade unions")]

There are four recognised campus [trade unions](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") at Durham with branches of the University and Colleges Union (UCU),[[207]](#cite_note-207) UNISON,[[208]](#cite_note-208) UNITE,[[209]](#cite_note-209) and GMB.[[210]](#cite_note-210) The four campus unions meet senior members of HR on a regular basis to negotiate on various matters.[[211]](#cite_note-211)

Union activity in the 2020s has included winning a £1000 payment to all staff and a number of other commitments in 2022,[[212]](#cite_note-212) and commitments from university management to become an accredited Real Living Wage employer, improvements in the starting salary grade for some staff and additional payments for international staff with visa costs in 2023.[[213]](#cite_note-213) There is a history of trades union activity at the university stretching back many decades with joint action by all staff at the university in 1996 being one example.[[214]](#cite_note-214)

### Departments and faculties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Departments and faculties")]

The teaching departments of the university are divided into four faculties: science, arts and humanities, social sciences, and business. Each faculty is headed by an executive dean and one or more deputies. These, along with the heads of the departments in the faculty and the vice-chancellor, make up the faculty board for that faculty.[[215]](#cite_note-215) Each department also has a board of studies consisting of the executive dean of their faculty, the teaching staff of the department, and student representatives.[[216]](#cite_note-216) Associated with the first three faculties are three combined honours degrees: natural sciences (BSc and MSci), liberal arts (BA), and combined honours in social sciences (BA).[[217]](#cite_note-217) Various joint honours degrees are also offered spanning multiple departments, such as the [philosophy, politics and economics](/wiki/Philosophy,_politics_and_economics "Philosophy, politics and economics") BA offered by the departments of philosophy, government and international affairs, and economics.[[218]](#cite_note-218)
