### Finances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Finances")]

In the financial year ending 31 July 2024, Durham had a total income of £513.4 million (2022/23 – £483.6 million) and total expenditure of £393 million (2022/23 – £488.7 million).[[223]](#cite_note-Durham_Financial_Statement_23/24-223) Key sources of income included £301.9 million from tuition fees and education contracts (2022/23 – £290 million), £48.2 million from funding body grants (2022/23 – £46.8 million), £60.7 million from research grants and contracts (2022/23 – £57.5 million), £5.8 million from investment income (2022/23 – £5.7 million) and £11.9 million from donations and endowments (2022/23 – £9.4 million).[[223]](#cite_note-Durham_Financial_Statement_23/24-223)

At year end, Durham had endowments of £106.6 million (2023 – £101.7 million) and total net assets of £544.3 million (2023 – £411 million). It holds the [fourteenth-largest endowment](/wiki/List_of_UK_universities_by_endowment "List of UK universities by endowment") of any university in the UK.[[223]](#cite_note-Durham_Financial_Statement_23/24-223)

## Academic Profile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Academic Profile")]

### Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Research")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/View_from_the_4th_floor_of_the_Calman_centre.jpg/250px-View_from_the_4th_floor_of_the_Calman_centre.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_from_the_4th_floor_of_the_Calman_centre.jpg)

The Dawson Building houses the departments of Archaeology and Anthropology

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Durham_Law_School.jpg/250px-Durham_Law_School.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_Law_School.jpg)

The Palatine Centre, home to [Durham Law School](/wiki/Durham_Law_School "Durham Law School")

The university is part of the [Russell Group](/wiki/Russell_Group "Russell Group") of British research universities as well as the European [Coimbra Group](/wiki/Coimbra_Group "Coimbra Group") and the global [Matariki Network of Universities](/wiki/Matariki_Network_of_Universities "Matariki Network of Universities").[[224]](#cite_note-global-224) It is also a partner in the Arqus European University Alliance, part of the European Union's [European Universities initiative](/wiki/European_Higher_Education_Area#European_Universities_initiative "European Higher Education Area"),[[225]](#cite_note-225) and is part of bilateral groups including the UK-Japan [RENKEI network](/wiki/British_Council#RENKEI_network "British Council"),[[226]](#cite_note-226) the UK-Norway North Sea University Partnership,[[227]](#cite_note-227) and the UK-Saudi Arabia Joint International Institute for Clean Hydrogen,[[228]](#cite_note-228) and other international research consortia including the [Virgo Consortium](/wiki/Virgo_Consortium "Virgo Consortium")[[229]](#cite_note-229) and the [University of the Arctic](/wiki/University_of_the_Arctic "University of the Arctic").[[230]](#cite_note-230) It is also part of regional groups including the [N8 Research Partnership](/wiki/N8_Research_Partnership "N8 Research Partnership") of universities in the north of England; Universities for North East England, bringing together the five universities in north east England;[[231]](#cite_note-231) the Northern Accelerator knowledge exchange and research commercialisation partnership;[[232]](#cite_note-232)[[233]](#cite_note-233) and the Durham Learning Alliance in association with four [further education colleges](/wiki/Further_education_college "Further education college") in County Durham.[[234]](#cite_note-234) In 2025, it became the first UK university to host a plenary meeting of the [European Research Council](/wiki/European_Research_Council "European Research Council") Scientific Council.[[235]](#cite_note-235)

Durham hosts the 'memory intensive' service of the UK's [tier-1](/wiki/Supercomputing_in_Europe#High_performance_computing_tiers "Supercomputing in Europe") [DiRAC](/wiki/DiRAC "DiRAC") supercomputer facility,[[236]](#cite_note-236) as well as the N8 Research Partnership's tier-2 Bede supercomputer.[[237]](#cite_note-237) It has partnered with the Net Zero Technology Centre, SHIFT Geothermal and the Reece Foundation to launch the UK National Geothermal Energy Centre,[[238]](#cite_note-238) and also leads the UK National Clean Maritime Research Hub, a consortium of thirteen universities.[[239]](#cite_note-239)

Research institutes at the university include the Biophysical Sciences Institute, the [Durham Energy Institute](/wiki/Durham_Energy_Institute "Durham Energy Institute"), the [Institute for Computational Cosmology](/wiki/Institute_for_Computational_Cosmology "Institute for Computational Cosmology"), the Institute for Data Science, the Institute for Medical Humanities, the Institute for Particle Physics Phenomenology, the [Institute of Advanced Study](/wiki/Institute_of_Advanced_Study_(Durham) "Institute of Advanced Study (Durham)"), the Institute of Hazard, Risk and Resiliance, the Institute of Medieval and Early Modern Studies, and the [Wolfson Research Institute for Health and Wellbeing](/wiki/Wolfson_Research_Institute "Wolfson Research Institute").[[240]](#cite_note-240) There are also a large number of research centres located within departments, including the [IBRU: Centre for Borders Research](/wiki/International_Boundaries_Research_Unit "International Boundaries Research Unit") (Department of Geography)[[241]](#cite_note-241) and the Durham Research in Economic Analysis and Mechanisms (Department of Economics), which has a research partnership with the [Competition and Markets Authority](/wiki/Competition_and_Markets_Authority "Competition and Markets Authority")'s Microeconomics Unit at the UK Government's Darlington Economic Campus.[[242]](#cite_note-242)

While Durham does not have a medical school, the "Health at Durham" programme takes in 44 institutes, centres, academies and projects from across the university. The programme focuses on non-clinical aspects of health, including physical, mental, social and environmental aspects of health.[[243]](#cite_note-243) Durham hosts the [Welcome Trust](/wiki/Welcome_Trust "Welcome Trust")-funded Black Health and the Humanities Network in the university's Institute of Medical Humanities, and is also one of the lead partners in the Northern Network for Medical Humanities Research.[[244]](#cite_note-244) The Durham-led Global Network for Neglected Tropical Diseases won the [Medical Research Council](/wiki/Medical_Research_Council_(United_Kingdom) "Medical Research Council (United Kingdom)")'s Impact Prize for Outstanding Team Impact in 2024.[[245]](#cite_note-245) The university was awarded a [Queen's Anniversary Prize](/wiki/Queen%27s_Anniversary_Prize "Queen's Anniversary Prize"), the UK's highest academic honour, in the medicine and health category in 2018 for "Influential research on parent/infant sleep",[[246]](#cite_note-246) in addition to one awarded in 1994 (the first year of the Queen's Anniversary Prizes) in the science and mathematics category for "Improving awareness of sciences and engineering in schools".[[247]](#cite_note-247)

In the 2021 [Research Excellence Framework](/wiki/Research_Excellence_Framework "Research Excellence Framework") (REF), Durham's research profile was assessed as 45 per cent world class (4\*) (33 per cent in 2014), 45 per cent internationally important (3\*) (50 per cent in 2014), 10 per cent internationally recognised (2\*) (15 per cent in 2014) and 0 per cent nationally recognised (1\*) (1 per cent in 2014).[[248]](#cite_note-THE_REF_2021-248)[[249]](#cite_note-THE_REF_2014-249) This gave it an overall 'GPA' (calculated by *Times Higher Education*) of 3.34 (3.14 in 2014, 2.72 in the 2008 [Research Assessment Exercise](/wiki/Research_Assessment_Exercise "Research Assessment Exercise")).[[248]](#cite_note-THE_REF_2021-248)[[249]](#cite_note-THE_REF_2014-249)[[250]](#cite_note-THE_RAE_2008-250) However, this improvement was in the context of a rise in the average profile, leading to a fall in Durham's relative ranking by GPA from 20th in 2014 to joint 25th in 2021.[[248]](#cite_note-THE_REF_2021-248) Durham's indexed research power (calculated by *Times Higher Education*, with the top university by research power having an index of 1000) rose from 282 in 2014 (relative to [UCL](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London")) to 299 in 2021 (relative to [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford")), and it remained ranked 20th by research power.[[248]](#cite_note-THE_REF_2021-248) Durham's 'market share' of funding (estimated by *Times Higher Education*) was expected to fall very slightly from 1.55 per cent in 2014 to 1.5 per cent in 2021.[[248]](#cite_note-THE_REF_2021-248) In regional terms, the success of Durham alongside [Newcastle University](/wiki/Newcastle_University "Newcastle University") and [Northumbria University](/wiki/Northumbria_University "Northumbria University") gave the North East the largest concentration of researchers in a city area outside of London.[[251]](#cite_note-251)[[252]](#cite_note-252)

### Reputation and rankings

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Durham_10_Years.png/330px-Durham_10_Years.png)](/wiki/File:Durham_10_Years.png)

Durham University's [national league table](/wiki/Rankings_of_universities_in_the_United_Kingdom "Rankings of universities in the United Kingdom") performance over the past ten years

Due to being one of the [oldest universities in the UK](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_the_United_Kingdom_by_date_of_foundation "List of universities in the United Kingdom by date of foundation") while also having an [Oxbridge](/wiki/Oxbridge "Oxbridge")-style collegiate structure[[259]](#cite_note-Abbott-259)[[260]](#cite_note-Dent-260) and a highly selective admissions process,[[261]](#cite_note-261) Durham has long been widely regarded as one of the UK's most elite universities,[[262]](#cite_note-262)[[263]](#cite_note-263)[[264]](#cite_note-264)[[265]](#cite_note-265)[[266]](#cite_note-266) albeit not as prestigious as Oxford or Cambridge.[[267]](#cite_note-BhopalMyers-267)[[259]](#cite_note-Abbott-259)[[260]](#cite_note-Dent-260)[[268]](#cite_note-268) It is also one of the few universities to have won *[University Challenge](/wiki/University_Challenge "University Challenge")* more than once (1977, 2000 and 2023),[[269]](#cite_note-269)[[270]](#cite_note-270) as well as the 2024–25 and 2025–26 *[Christmas University Challenge](/wiki/Christmas_University_Challenge "Christmas University Challenge")* (for alumni), the first university to win in successive years.[[271]](#cite_note-UC_Christmas_Special_2024-271)[[272]](#cite_note-UC_Christmas_Special_2025-272) Durham was also [*Sunday Times* University of the Year](/wiki/Sunday_Times_University_of_the_Year "Sunday Times University of the Year") for 2005 and 2026.[[273]](#cite_note-273)[[274]](#cite_note-274)[[275]](#cite_note-UoY2026-275)

Durham University's Strategic Plan 2017–2027 defined targets of being in the top five nationally on the Times/Sunday Times league table, of having 50 per cent of eligible subjects in the top 50 globally on the QS world rankings, and of being in the top three UK institutions by citations per academic staff member.[[276]](#cite_note-276)

National

Durham regularly places in the top five in [rankings of universities in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Rankings_of_universities_in_the_United_Kingdom "Rankings of universities in the United Kingdom") and is one of only four universities (along with [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford"), [Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") and [St Andrews](/wiki/University_of_St_Andrews "University of St Andrews")) to have not left the top 10 in any of the three main domestic league tables since 2013.[[a]](#cite_note-277) The university was ranked fifth overall in the *[Complete University Guide](/wiki/Complete_University_Guide "Complete University Guide")* 2026[[253]](#cite_note-Complete_League_Table-253) and the *Guardian University Guide* 2026,[[254]](#cite_note-The_Guardian_University_Guide-254) and third overall in the *Times and Sunday Times Good University Guide*, placing it above Oxford and Cambridge and marking the first time in the guide’s 32-year history that neither of the Oxbridge universities were ranked in its top three.[[255]](#cite_note-The_Times_and_Sunday_Times_University_Guide-255)[[277]](#cite_note-278) In addition, the *Times and Sunday Times Good University Guide* 2026 named Durham as University of the Year and as University of the Year in the North and Northeast, as well as runner-up for University of the Year for Graduate Employment.[[275]](#cite_note-UoY2026-275)

The *High Fliers Research* UK graduate market report for 2026 placed Durham 14th in its table of universities targeted by the largest number of top employees.[[278]](#cite_note-highfliers2026-279)

In the 2025 *[Complete University Guide](/wiki/Complete_University_Guide "Complete University Guide")* subject rankings, Durham had 31 of 33 subjects ranked in the top ten.[[253]](#cite_note-Complete_League_Table-253) In *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* 2025 subject rankings, Durham had 21 of 28 subjects ranked in the top ten.[[254]](#cite_note-The_Guardian_University_Guide-254)

International

The *[Times Higher Education World University Rankings](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_University_Rankings "Times Higher Education World University Rankings")* for 2026 placed Durham 175th. In subject area rankings for 2026, Durham was placed 40th for arts and humanities, 55th for law, 94th for education studies and 101-105th for psychology and physical sciences. Durham also ranked 34th in the Times Higher Education impact ranking for 2025, measuring the impact of universities' research, stewardship, outreach and teaching towards the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations")' [Sustainable Development Goals](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goals "Sustainable Development Goals").[[279]](#cite_note-THE_Durham-280)

The *Shanghai [Academic Ranking of World Universities](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities")* for 2025 placed Durham in the 201-300 range.[[280]](#cite_note-281)

Durham University has maintained a *[QS World University Ranking](/wiki/QS_World_University_Ranking "QS World University Ranking")* in the top 100 since 2010.[[281]](#cite_note-282) The *[QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings") 2026*[[282]](#cite_note-283) placed Durham 94th in the world, while the QS European University Rankings 2025[[283]](#cite_note-284) placed Durham 32nd in Europe. In the *QS World University Ranking by Subject 2025*, Durham was ranked in the top ten in three subjects (archaeology; theology, divinity and religious studies; and classics and ancient history), in the top 50 in a further six subjects and in the top 100 in a further 13 subjects.[[284]](#cite_note-285)

Durham's student body consists of 16,560 undergraduates and 4,590 postgraduate students (2024/25).[[5]](#cite_note-HESA_citation-5) The university is consistently designated as a 'high-tariff' institution by the [Department for Education](/wiki/Department_for_Education "Department for Education"), with the average undergraduate entrant to the university in recent years amassing between 157–160 [UCAS Tariff](/wiki/UCAS_Tariff "UCAS Tariff") points in their top three pre-university qualifications – the equivalent of A\*AA to A\*A\*A at [A-Level](/wiki/A-level_(United_Kingdom) "A-level (United Kingdom)").[[287]](#cite_note-Mean_Tariff-291) Based on 2022/23 [HESA](/wiki/Higher_Education_Statistics_Agency "Higher Education Statistics Agency") entry standards data published in domestic league tables, which include a broad range of qualifications beyond the top three exam grades, the average student at Durham University achieved 181 points – the 11th highest in the country.[[286]](#cite_note-CUG_Entry-289)

The university gave offers of admission to 48% of its undergraduate applicants in 2022, the 13th lowest offer rate across the country.[[288]](#cite_note-292) In the 2023–24 academic year, 31 per cent of students (across all levels of study) came from outside the UK and 54 per cent of students were women.[[289]](#cite_note-293)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Cosin%27s_Hall%2C_Durham.jpg/250px-Cosin%27s_Hall%2C_Durham.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cosin%27s_Hall,_Durham.jpg)

[Cosin's Hall](/wiki/Cosin%27s_Hall "Cosin's Hall"), home to the [Institute of Advanced Study](/wiki/Institute_of_Advanced_Study_(Durham) "Institute of Advanced Study (Durham)")

Durham was criticised in 2017 for not accepting as many students from low participation neighbourhoods, and from state schools, as might be expected from its admission standards and course offerings. For admissions in 2015/16 (the data published in 2017 that sparked the criticism), Durham had the third lowest percentage of state school students (among higher education institutions with over 1,000 full-time first-degree entrants) at 60.5 per cent, compared to a [Higher Education Statistics Agency](/wiki/Higher_Education_Statistics_Agency "Higher Education Statistics Agency") benchmark of 75 per cent. According to pro-vice-chancellor, Alan Hudson, this was a temporary drop from the 63 per cent level the university has reached in recent years, and to which it was expected to return in 2016/17. The university also fell short of its benchmark for admissions from low participation neighbourhoods, accepting 5.1 per cent, compared to a benchmark of 6 per cent.[[291]](#cite_note-297)[[292]](#cite_note-298) The data for 2016/17 showed that admissions from state schools had recovered to 62.9 per cent, still short of the location-adjusted benchmark of 74.9 per cent, and that admissions from low participation neighbourhoods were 5.2 per cent compared to the location-adjusted benchmark of 6.6 per cent.[[293]](#cite_note-299)

Since 1992 the university has run a widening access programme, originally called the Centre for Lifelong Learning and now known as the Durham Centre for Academic Development. The centre provides access to Durham degrees for mature students who show academic promise but do not hold the traditional entry requirements. The centre runs a range of [foundation year](/wiki/Foundation_year "Foundation year") courses associated with specific degree courses.[[294]](#cite_note-300)

Durham has partnered with the [Sutton Trust](/wiki/Sutton_Trust "Sutton Trust") since 2012 to run the Durham University Sutton Trust Summer School for [gifted and talented](/wiki/Gifted_education#United_Kingdom "Gifted education") school children from underrepresented backgrounds, leading to qualification with 16 to 32 [UCAS Tariff](/wiki/UCAS_Tariff "UCAS Tariff") points and a guaranteed conditional offer from Durham if they choose to apply.[[295]](#cite_note-301)[[296]](#cite_note-302) The university also runs the Durham International Summer School[[297]](#cite_note-303) and partners with the Sutton Trust to run the Durham Teacher Summer School.[[298]](#cite_note-304)

In 2014, Durham became the first UK university to participate in the [Inside-Out Prison Exchange Program](/wiki/Inside-Out_Prison_Exchange_Program "Inside-Out Prison Exchange Program").[[299]](#cite_note-305) The scheme, where students study alongside inmates, ran in [Durham Prison](/wiki/Durham_Prison "Durham Prison") and the high-security [Frankland Prison](/wiki/HM_Prison_Frankland "HM Prison Frankland") in 2015 and was expanded to include [Low Newton Prison](/wiki/HM_Prison_Low_Newton "HM Prison Low Newton") in 2016.[[300]](#cite_note-306)[[301]](#cite_note-307)

Durham gives a bursary, known as the Durham Grant, to home undergraduate and PGCE students from low and middle income households, including all students receiving a means-tested maintenance loan. As of 2024,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit) the maximum is £2,670 for students with a household income (as assessed by [Student Finance England](/wiki/Student_Finance_England "Student Finance England")) of £30,000 or less, falling to a minimum bursary of £700 for students with a household income between £47,201 and £62,342.[[302]](#cite_note-308)[[303]](#cite_note-309) The university also runs the "Supported Progression" programme for sixth-form students, aimed at helping talented young people from the North East, Cumbria and Yorkshire to fulfill their potential via a two-year structured programme of events.[[304]](#cite_note-310)

For UK domiciled young full-time undergraduate entrants in 2020/21, 61.6% came from state schools, significantly below the location-adjusted benchmark of 78.5%, and 7.6% came from low participation neighbourhoods, not significantly different from the location-adjusted benchmark of 8.0%.[[305]](#cite_note-311) For UK-domiciled undergraduate entrants in 2022/23, UCAS data shows no significant difference in offer rate with gender, a statistically significantly higher offer rate for Black and Asian applicants coupled with a significantly lower offer rate for white applicants,
and a statistically significantly higher offer rate for applicants from the 80% of neighbourhoods with the lowest rates of participation in higher education coupled with a significantly lower offer rate for applicants from the 20% of neighbourhoods with the highest rates of participation in higher education.[[306]](#cite_note-UCAS_reports_2022-312)

## Student Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Student Life")]

See also: [List of social activities at Durham University](/wiki/List_of_social_activities_at_Durham_University "List of social activities at Durham University")

### College life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: College life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Great_Hall.jpg/250px-Great_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Great_Hall.jpg)

The [Great Hall](/wiki/University_College,_Durham#Great_Hall "University College, Durham") at [University College](/wiki/University_College,_Durham "University College, Durham") – communal dining is traditional at most Durham colleges

Durham students belong to a college for the duration of their time at the university. Most students live in their college for the first year of their undergraduate life, then choose to 'live-out' in their second year, and subsequently have the option of moving back into college for their final year, usually via a ballot system.[[307]](#cite_note-DSU:_QAA_Student_Written_Submission-313) The colleges provide most of the pastoral care and social centre of students with each running a college tutorial system,[[307]](#cite_note-DSU:_QAA_Student_Written_Submission-313)[[308]](#cite_note-Colleges:_Support_Structure-314) along with [junior common rooms](/wiki/Junior_common_room "Junior common room") (JCRs) running events, providing representation and supporting societies for undergraduate members, [middle common rooms](/wiki/Middle_common_room "Middle common room") (MCRs) for postgraduate students and [senior common rooms](/wiki/Senior_common_room "Senior common room") (SCRs) for college officers, fellows and tutors. JCRs and MCRs are run by executive committees, usually headed by a president although some colleges use [other titles](/wiki/Senior_man "Senior man").[[309]](#cite_note-315)[[310]](#cite_note-316)

Each college has a unique identity and a variety of facilities for students ranging from computer rooms and libraries to tennis courts and gyms.[[311]](#cite_note-317) Most colleges have their own sports teams and compete in the collegiate leagues (such as [Durham College Rowing](/wiki/Durham_College_Rowing "Durham College Rowing")) and may also have their own theatre and music societies, which operate in parallel to the university-level sports teams and organisations.[[312]](#cite_note-Colleges:_Sports_and_societies-318)

### Student organisations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Student organisations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Durham_Union_Palace_Green.jpg/250px-Durham_Union_Palace_Green.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_Union_Palace_Green.jpg)

The [Durham Union Society](/wiki/Durham_Union_Society "Durham Union Society") is the university's largest student society

There are over 300 student clubs and organisations at Durham, covering a wide range of interests.[[313]](#cite_note-319) [Durham Students' Union](/wiki/Durham_Students%27_Union "Durham Students' Union") (DSU) charters and provides most of the funding for these organisations. The [Durham Union Society](/wiki/Durham_Union_Society "Durham Union Society"), founded in 1842 as Durham's Student Debating & Union Society, claims to be the largest independent student society in Durham, and hosts weekly debates and addresses from invited guests.[[314]](#cite_note-dunion-320) The Durham University History Society is the oldest academic society at the university, founded in 1926.[[315]](#cite_note-321)

### Diversity and inclusion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Diversity and inclusion")]

Black, Asian, Minority and Ethnic (BAME) students made up 32.6% of full-time Durham students in 2019/20, although students who classified themselves as 'black' numbered only 382 out of 18,430 full-time students (2.07%).[[319]](#cite_note-328) A 2018 article for *[The Tab](/wiki/The_Tab "The Tab")* said that the low representation of black students meant that support structures for many young, vulnerable, black students were non-existent.[[320]](#cite_note-329)

Incidents of racism, sexism and social elitism have been reported at Durham University.[[321]](#cite_note-330)[[322]](#cite_note-331)[[323]](#cite_note-332)[[324]](#cite_note-333) The university has stated that they condemn all racism and hate crime.[[325]](#cite_note-334) The university established an independent commission, the Durham Commission on Respect, Values and Behaviours, in October 2018. The report of this commission was published in July 2020, highlighting that there were multiple problems with bullying, discrimination and a lack of diversity, and that many students came to the university with a "sense of entitlement". The report also found that the lack of diversity was "at the root of a number of discriminatory and exclusionary behaviours", including racism, sexism, and disrespect of working class students. The commission made 20 recommendations, all of which were accepted by the university's management.[[326]](#cite_note-335)

The university joined the Race Equality Charter in 2019 and received a bronze award in 2022.[[327]](#cite_note-336) It launched an 'Inclusive Durham' framework in 2024 and in 2025 signed the Gypsies, Travellers, Roma, Showmen and Boaters into Higher Education Pledge and received an institutional [Athena SWAN](/wiki/Athena_SWAN "Athena SWAN") silver award (having held an institutional bronze award since 2018).[[328]](#cite_note-337)[[329]](#cite_note-338)[[330]](#cite_note-339) The university has also been a [Stonewall](/wiki/Stonewall_(charity) "Stonewall (charity)") Diversity Champion since 2012.[[331]](#cite_note-340)

### Civic engagement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Civic engagement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Dunelm3.jpg/250px-Dunelm3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dunelm3.jpg)

Dunelm House, home of the [Durham Students' Union](/wiki/Durham_Students%27_Union "Durham Students' Union")

Durham University Student Volunteering and Outreach (DUSVO; formally Student Community Action – SCA), was formed in 1989 and, As of 2020,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit) oversaw over 80 volunteer projects in Durham and the surrounding area, (including over 50 student-run projects),[[332]](#cite_note-StudentsDUSVO-341) involving more than 2,000 students yearly. DUSVO runs projects through online portals for Staff and Students respectively. Staff at the university are permitted to spend up to 5 days (35 hours) volunteering during working time.[[333]](#cite_note-StaffDUSVO-342) It was awarded the [Queen's Award for Voluntary Service](/wiki/Queen%27s_Award_for_Voluntary_Service "Queen's Award for Voluntary Service") – the UK's highest honour for volunteer groups – in 2020.[[334]](#cite_note-qavs-343) The annual Durham University Volunteering Awards recognise individuals, teams, colleges and projects across several categories.[[335]](#cite_note-AwardsDUSVO-344)

Durham University Charity Kommittee (or DUCK) is the university's student [rag week](/wiki/Rag_week "Rag week")[[336]](#cite_note-Community_Activities-345) and the fundraising arm of the [Durham Students Union](/wiki/Durham_Students_Union "Durham Students Union"). Originally a week-long event, DUCK now has events raising money for local or national charities throughout the year. Activities and organisation happen both at the university level and within colleges.[[337]](#cite_note-DUCK:_Get_Involved!-346) DUCK has previously organised expeditions to the Himalayas,[[338]](#cite_note-DUCK:_Expeditions-347) Jordan[[338]](#cite_note-DUCK:_Expeditions-347) and Mount Kilimanjaro[[338]](#cite_note-DUCK:_Expeditions-347) and been involved in the university-run 'Project Sri Lanka'[[339]](#cite_note-Project_Sri_Lanka-348) and 'Project Thailand'.[[340]](#cite_note-Project_Thailand-349)

Team Durham Community Outreach is a sports community programme aimed at giving support and opportunities through the use of sport.[[336]](#cite_note-Community_Activities-345) The programme runs projects such as summer camps for children from the Youth Engagement Service and fostered backgrounds along with providing coaching at local schools as well as participating in sports in action.[[341]](#cite_note-Sports_in_Action-350)

### Town and gown relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Town and gown relations")]

The relationship between [the university and the wider city](/wiki/Town_and_gown "Town and gown") has not always been free of tension.[[342]](#cite_note-351)[[343]](#cite_note-352) University plans for expansion have also faced local opposition,[[344]](#cite_note-353)[[345]](#cite_note-354) and there were claims when the 2017–2027 strategy was launched that the university had ignored concerns raised about its expansion at the Durham University Residents' Forum.[[346]](#cite_note-355)

The university formed a Community Response Team in 2020 to support [Durham Constabulary](/wiki/Durham_Constabulary "Durham Constabulary") during Covid-19. This now responds to complaints of anti-social noise at student properties between 9:30pm and 3:30am, via the [Police 101](/wiki/Police_101 "Police 101") non-emergency contact number, and also has patrols in student areas.[[347]](#cite_note-356) The university also has two Student Community Wardens, intended to provide guidance to students living out of college, to represent students on the Durham University Residents' Forum, and to liaise with college common rooms, Durham Students' Union, Police University Liaison and council Neighbourhood Wardens.[[348]](#cite_note-357)

Durham University and [Durham County Council](/wiki/Durham_County_Council "Durham County Council") are both members of the International Town & Gown Association.[[349]](#cite_note-358)

### Student media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Student media")]

*[Palatinate](/wiki/Palatinate_(newspaper) "Palatinate (newspaper)")*, Durham's independent student-run fortnightly newspaper, has been continually published since 1948.[[350]](#cite_note-Echo-359) Notable former editors include [George Alagiah](/wiki/George_Alagiah "George Alagiah"),[[350]](#cite_note-Echo-359) [Hunter Davies](/wiki/Hunter_Davies "Hunter Davies"),[[351]](#cite_note-360) [Piers Merchant](/wiki/Piers_Merchant "Piers Merchant"),[[352]](#cite_note-361) [Sir Timothy Laurence](/wiki/Timothy_Laurence "Timothy Laurence"),[[353]](#cite_note-362) [Jeremy Vine](/wiki/Jeremy_Vine "Jeremy Vine")[[350]](#cite_note-Echo-359) and [Harold Evans](/wiki/Harold_Evans "Harold Evans").[[350]](#cite_note-Echo-359) Durham's student television station ([PalTV](/wiki/PalTV "PalTV")) is a section of Palatinate and won Broadcaster of the Year at the [National Student Television Association](/wiki/National_Student_Television_Association "National Student Television Association") awards in 2022, 2023 and 2024.[[354]](#cite_note-363)

[Purple Radio](/wiki/Purple_Radio "Purple Radio") is Durham's student radio station. It broadcasts live from the [DSU](/wiki/Durham_Students%27_Union "Durham Students' Union") 24 hours a day during term time. The station has existed since the 1980s and is a recognised DSU society. Two daily news bulletins are broadcast every weekday, as well as a Breakfast Show and an Evening Show.[[355]](#cite_note-Purple_Radio_Schedule-364)

The Bubble, founded in 2010, is an online magazine based at the university covering various subjects, including student and university news.[[356]](#cite_note-365)

[The Tab](/wiki/The_Tab "The Tab") established a Durham edition in 2012, as part of its initial launch as a national student paper.[[357]](#cite_note-366)

### Sports teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Sports teams")]

Main article: [Team Durham](/wiki/Team_Durham "Team Durham")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Durham_regatta_Univ_College_Durham_v%27s_Newcastle_Uni.jpg/250px-Durham_regatta_Univ_College_Durham_v%27s_Newcastle_Uni.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_regatta_Univ_College_Durham_v%27s_Newcastle_Uni.jpg)

[University College Boat Club](/wiki/University_College_Boat_Club_(Durham) "University College Boat Club (Durham)") and Newcastle University racing at [Durham Regatta](/wiki/Durham_Regatta "Durham Regatta")

Sport at Durham is a key aspect of student life with the vast majority of students regularly taking part at both a university and college level. It has twice been named *Times* and *Sunday Times* Sports University of the Year, in 2015 and 2023.[[358]](#cite_note-367)[[359]](#cite_note-368)[[360]](#cite_note-Sports_Uni_2015-369) As of 2018, the university caters for more than 50 different sports, organised under the umbrella of [Team Durham](/wiki/Team_Durham "Team Durham"), with extensive sports facilities at the [Maiden Castle sports centre](/wiki/Maiden_Castle_sports_centre "Maiden Castle sports centre") and [the Racecourse](/wiki/The_Racecourse "The Racecourse").[[361]](#cite_note-370)[[362]](#cite_note-371)[[363]](#cite_note-Team_Durham:_Facilities-372)

The university is recognised as a [Centre of Cricketing Excellence](/wiki/Durham_MCC_University "Durham MCC University"),[[364]](#cite_note-373) as a [British Rowing](/wiki/British_Rowing "British Rowing") Performance Centre,[[365]](#cite_note-374) and as a [Lawn Tennis Association](/wiki/Lawn_Tennis_Association "Lawn Tennis Association") University Partner.[[366]](#cite_note-375) It is also a [Football Association](/wiki/Football_Association "Football Association") Football Accredited University, with the highest 3\* rating.[[367]](#cite_note-376)

Durham has been in the top three across all sports in the [British Universities & Colleges Sport](/wiki/British_Universities_%26_Colleges_Sport "British Universities & Colleges Sport") (BUCS) table since 2011/12. In 2023/24, it won 13 BUCS national championships across 11 sports.[[368]](#cite_note-377)

[Durham University Boat Club](/wiki/Durham_University_Boat_Club "Durham University Boat Club") has a good record at the [BUCS Regatta](/wiki/University_rowing_(UK) "University rowing (UK)"), having won the title for ten consecutive years (2004–2013) before coming second in 2014, then regaining the title in 2015[[369]](#cite_note-Stage_is_set_for_rowing_showdown-378) and again in 2023.[[370]](#cite_note-379) It also competes in [Durham Regatta](/wiki/Durham_Regatta "Durham Regatta") and [the Boat Race of the North](/wiki/The_Boat_Race_of_the_North "The Boat Race of the North") against Newcastle University, which ran 1997 – 2010 and was revived in 2015.[[371]](#cite_note-380)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Durham_University_Racecourse.JPG/250px-Durham_University_Racecourse.JPG)](/wiki/File:Durham_University_Racecourse.JPG)

[The Racecourse](/wiki/The_Racecourse "The Racecourse") is one of the university's main sites for sporting facilities

Durham colleges compete with colleges from the [University of York](/wiki/University_of_York "University of York") in the annual College Varsity tournament held since 2014.[[372]](#cite_note-381) Durham won this tournament in 2014 (in York) and 2015 (in Durham) before York recorded their first victory in 2016 (in York). Durham's BUCS matches against [Loughborough University](/wiki/Loughborough_University "Loughborough University") have been described as the 'BUCS Varsity', and a varsity competition between Durham colleges and Loughborough halls of residence has been held since 2015/16.[[373]](#cite_note-382)

Palatinates (named after the colour associated with the university) are the university's [sporting colours](/wiki/Sporting_colours "Sporting colours"), given to athletes who demonstrate a very high standard (such as international representation) in their sport. Earning a full palatinate has been described by the university as a 'notoriously difficult' achievement.[[374]](#cite_note-pawards2020-383) In 2022 just 12 student athletes received the full award, with a further 53 earning a half-palatinate.[[375]](#cite_note-384)

Esports are included in Team Durham through Durham University Esports and Gaming (DUEG) as part of Team Durham. DUEG participates in 12 games, across National Student Esports (NSE) and National University Esports League (NUEL) tournaments.[[376]](#cite_note-Esports-385)

### Music and drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Music and drama")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Durham_University_Botanic_Garden.jpg/250px-Durham_University_Botanic_Garden.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_University_Botanic_Garden.jpg)

[Durham University Botanic Garden](/wiki/Durham_University_Botanic_Garden "Durham University Botanic Garden")

The central body for theatre at the university is [Durham Student Theatre](/wiki/Durham_Student_Theatre "Durham Student Theatre") (DST), with 33 student-run theatre societies as of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit), including university-wide companies, college-based companies and two Durham Students' Union theatre societies. These perform in the university's [Assembly Rooms Theatre](/wiki/Assembly_Rooms_Theatre_(Durham) "Assembly Rooms Theatre (Durham)"), in college venues, and in other venues around Durham, as well as at the Durham Drama Festival, the Durham Fringe Festival and the [Edinburgh Fringe Festival](/wiki/Edinburgh_Fringe_Festival "Edinburgh Fringe Festival").[[377]](#cite_note-386)[[378]](#cite_note-387)

Since 1975, the university has played host to the Durham Drama Festival which celebrates new theatrical and dramatic material written by Durham students.[[379]](#cite_note-388)

The Durham Revue is the university's [sketch comedy](/wiki/Sketch_comedy "Sketch comedy") group. Tracing its roots back to the early 1950s, and known under its current name since 1988, the group consists of six writer-performers (auditioned, interviewed and chosen each Michaelmas Term) and produces a series of shows each year. The group performs annually with [Cambridge University](/wiki/Cambridge_University "Cambridge University")'s [Footlights](/wiki/Footlights "Footlights") and [Oxford University](/wiki/Oxford_University "Oxford University")'s [The Oxford Revue](/wiki/The_Oxford_Revue "The Oxford Revue"), as well as at the [Edinburgh Fringe Festival](/wiki/Edinburgh_Fringe_Festival "Edinburgh Fringe Festival").[[380]](#cite_note-389)

The Durham Cathedral Choir offers choral scholarships to male and female students, and several of the colleges (University, Hatfield, Hild Bede, St John's, St Chad's, St Cuthbert's, Grey and St Mary's) also offer organ and/or choral scholarships, as does the Catholic Chaplaincy.[[381]](#cite_note-390) [Northern Lights](/wiki/List_of_university_a_cappella_groups_in_the_United_Kingdom#Northern_Lights "List of university a cappella groups in the United Kingdom"), Durham's student [a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella") group, won the UK finals of the [International Championship of Collegiate A Cappella](/wiki/International_Championship_of_Collegiate_A_Cappella "International Championship of Collegiate A Cappella") in 2023, 2024 and 2025 going on to compete in the world finals, where they ranked third in 2025.[[382]](#cite_note-391)[[383]](#cite_note-392)[[384]](#cite_note-ICCA2025-393)[[385]](#cite_note-394)[[386]](#cite_note-395)

Durham is also home to the oldest [Gamelan](/wiki/Gamelan "Gamelan") [slendro](/wiki/Slendro "Slendro") set in the UK with an active community group and an artist in residence. The instruments are currently housed in the Grade II listed [Durham University Observatory](/wiki/Durham_University_Observatory "Durham University Observatory").[[387]](#cite_note-396) Recently a set tuned to peloghas been added meaning that Durham now has a complete Gamelan orchestra. In recent years, the Durham Gamelan Society has performed at several major public events such as the Gong Festivals 2011 & 2012[[388]](#cite_note-397) and at the Gamelan Lokananta all night *[wayang kulit](/wiki/Wayang_kulit "Wayang kulit")* in celebration of York University's Gamelan Sekar Petak 30th anniversary in 2012.[[389]](#cite_note-398)

### Leadership and personal development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Leadership and personal development")]

Students can participate in several personal development courses offered by the university. The Durham Leadership framework aims to develop student leaders and includes online resources, an Emerging Leadership Program and a year-long Leadership Academy.[[390]](#cite_note-LeadershipFramework-399) The Laidlaw Research and Leadership Program, which is provided at multiple universities as part of the Laidlaw Foundation,[[391]](#cite_note-400) provides funding for 25 Durham Undergraduates. The program comprises a 6-week summer research project, a leadership training retreat, a 6-week summer 'leadership-in-action experience' and ethical masterclass.[[392]](#cite_note-401) The university also offers the Durham Inspired Award for skills and experience gained outside of formal academic programmes.[[393]](#cite_note-402)

## Alumni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Alumni")]

### Societies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_University&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Societies")]

Durham alumni are active through organisations and events such as the annual reunions, dinners and balls. By 2024 there were over 230,000 Durham graduates in the Durham alumni community.[[394]](#cite_note-403) Every November, "Dunelm Days" events are held by alumni groups around the world.[[395]](#cite_note-404)

The umbrella organisation for Durham University alumni is Dunelm, which offers a range of events and dedicated alumni services.[[396]](#cite_note-405) Dunelm can trace its roots to the Durham University Society, formed in 1921, the Society of Dunelmians in 1905 and the Durham University Association in 1866.[[397]](#cite_note-406) Dunelm USA, formerly the North American Foundation for the University of Durham or NAFUD, is a philanthropic body in the United States that hosts alumni events and fundraises for Durham-related projects.[[398]](#cite_note-407)

A [masonic lodge](/wiki/Masonic_lodge "Masonic lodge"), University of Durham Lodge no. 3030, was founded in 1904 for university alumni and meets at [Freemasons' Hall](/wiki/Freemasons%27_Hall,_London "Freemasons' Hall, London") in [Covent Garden](/wiki/Covent_Garden "Covent Garden").[[399]](#cite_note-408)

In politics, 14 Durham alumni and former staff were elected to the UK parliament in the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election") (eight [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), two [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), two [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Ireland](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Northern_Ireland "Secretary of State for Northern Ireland") at the time of the [Good Friday Peace Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Peace_Agreement "Good Friday Peace Agreement").[[402]](#cite_note-Mo_Mowlam-411) Notable figures in law have included two [Supreme Court justices](/wiki/Justice_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Justice of the Supreme Court of the United Kingdom"), [Anthony Hughes](/wiki/Anthony_Hughes,_Lord_Hughes_of_Ombersley "Anthony Hughes, Lord Hughes of Ombersley")[[403]](#cite_note-412) and [Jill Black](/wiki/Jill_Black,_Lady_Black_of_Derwent "Jill Black, Lady Black of Derwent"),[[404]](#cite_note-413) and a [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor"), [Robert Buckland](/wiki/Robert_Buckland "Robert Buckland").[[405]](#cite_note-414) Alumni and staff in religion have included two [archbishops of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"), [Michael Ramsey](/wiki/Michael_Ramsey "Michael Ramsey")[[406]](#cite_note-415) and [Justin Welby](/wiki/Justin_Welby "Justin Welby"),[[407]](#cite_note-416) as well as [Libby Lane](/wiki/Libby_Lane "Libby Lane"), the first woman bishop in the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England").[[408]](#cite_note-Libby_Lane-417) Durham graduates in the military have included two [chiefs of the general staff](/wiki/Chief_of_the_General_Staff_(United_Kingdom) "Chief of the General Staff (United Kingdom)") (professional heads of the British Army), [Richard Dannatt](/wiki/Richard_Dannatt,_Baron_Dannatt "Richard Dannatt, Baron Dannatt")[[409]](#cite_note-Richard_Dannatt-418) and [Mark Carleton-Smith](/wiki/Mark_Carleton-Smith "Mark Carleton-Smith").[[410]](#cite_note-419)

In science, Durham graduates include cosmologist [John D. Barrow](/wiki/John_D._Barrow "John D. Barrow"), winner of the [Templeton Prize](/wiki/Templeton_Prize "Templeton Prize");[[411]](#cite_note-John_D._Barrow-420) particle physicist [George Rochester](/wiki/George_Rochester "George Rochester"), co-discoverer of the [kaon](/wiki/Kaon "Kaon");[[412]](#cite_note-George_Rochester-421) geophysicist and statistician [Harold Jeffreys](/wiki/Harold_Jeffreys "Harold Jeffreys"), winner of the [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society")'s [Copley Medal](/wiki/Copley_Medal "Copley Medal");[[413]](#cite_note-Harold_Jeffreys-422) geologist [Kingsley Charles Dunham](/wiki/Kingsley_Charles_Dunham "Kingsley Charles Dunham"), director of the [British Geological Survey](/wiki/British_Geological_Survey "British Geological Survey");[[414]](#cite_note-423) and astrophysicist [Rosemary Coogan](/wiki/Rosemary_Coogan "Rosemary Coogan"), [European Space Agency astronaut](/wiki/European_Astronaut_Corps "European Astronaut Corps").[[415]](#cite_note-424) Alumni [Traidcraft](/wiki/Traidcraft "Traidcraft"),[[416]](#cite_note-Richard_John_Adams-425) and [Tim Smit](/wiki/Tim_Smit "Tim Smit"), co-founder of the [Eden Project](/wiki/Eden_Project "Eden Project").[[417]](#cite_note-426) Media figures have included [Harold Evans](/wiki/Harold_Evans "Harold Evans"), editor of *[The Sunday Times](/wiki/The_Sunday_Times_(UK) "The Sunday Times (UK)")* and *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")*;[[418]](#cite_note-427) [Biddy Baxter](/wiki/Biddy_Baxter "Biddy Baxter"), producer of *[Blue Peter](/wiki/Blue_Peter "Blue Peter")*;[[419]](#cite_note-428) and television presenters [George Alagiah](/wiki/George_Alagiah "George Alagiah"),[[420]](#cite_note-429) [Gabby Logan](/wiki/Gabby_Logan "Gabby Logan")[[421]](#cite_note-430) and [Jeremy Vine](/wiki/Jeremy_Vine "Jeremy Vine").[[422]](#cite_note-431) Noted writers include [Edward Bradley](/wiki/Edward_Bradley_(writer) "Edward Bradley (writer)"), author of *[The Adventures of Mr. Verdant Green](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Mr._Verdant_Green "The Adventures of Mr. Verdant Green")*,[[423]](#cite_note-432) and [Hunter Davies](/wiki/Hunter_Davies "Hunter Davies"), author of *[The Beatles: The Authorised Biography](/wiki/The_Beatles:_The_Authorised_Biography "The Beatles: The Authorised Biography")*.[[424]](#cite_note-433)

In sports, Durham has produced more professional athletes than any other British university.[[425]](#cite_note-434) Sporting alumni have include England rugby captains [Will Carling](/wiki/Will_Carling "Will Carling")[[426]](#cite_note-Sports_Alumni-435) and [Phil de Glanville](/wiki/Phil_de_Glanville "Phil de Glanville"),[[426]](#cite_note-Sports_Alumni-435) England cricket captains [Nasser Hussain](/wiki/Nasser_Hussain "Nasser Hussain")[[426]](#cite_note-Sports_Alumni-435) and [Andrew Strauss](/wiki/Andrew_Strauss "Andrew Strauss"),[[427]](#cite_note-Andrew_Strauss-436) and Olympic gold medallists [Jonathan Edwards](/wiki/Jonathan_Edwards_(triple_jumper) "Jonathan Edwards (triple jumper)")[[428]](#cite_note-Jonathan_Edwards-437) and [Sophie Hosking](/wiki/Sophie_Hosking "Sophie Hosking").[[429]](#cite_note-438)
